issue_title: Soak stdin to file if encoding is not utf8
Switch docs to Python 3 syntax by default
issue_body:
The docs currently use `sigtools.modifiers` decorators to augment parameters with annotations or to make them keyword-only. I'm suspecting this distracts users from the examples' main points.

Given:

* The above,
* That new python users are increasingly being directed to Python 3,
* That most major packages work on it,
* That Debian and Ubuntu both intend to remove Python 2 from their default installation,

I suggest adjusting the examples so they make use of keyword-only parameters and annotations, and using "note" sections destined to users stuck with Python 2 or trying to keep compatibility with it.

issue_title: Make socket an object attribute by default instead of a class attribute
issue_body:
(As per owners request, I'll open this kind of discussion in a new issue.)
In `ServerConnection` and `DCCConection`, `socket` is initialized as a class attribute, which later masked by an object attribute of the same name in `connect` method. I think it's better to initialize it as an object attribute since `self.socket` is not supposed to be shared among different instances. Set uninitialized attributes to `None` is the best practice if it is not shared among instances https://stackoverflow.com/a/31467178

I think `socket` should be initialized in `__init__`:

```
def __init__(self, reactor):
	super(ServerConnection, self).__init__(reactor)
	self.connected = False
	self.features = features.FeatureSet()
	self.socket = None
```

issue_title: irc.bot.get_version() fails with exception.
issue_body:
The relevant exception is


```
#!python

TypeError: sequence item 0: expected string, int found
```

What happens is that irc.client.VERSION is a tuple of integers. In fact, it is tested that this is the case.

Problem is that bot.get_version() tries to do


```
#!python

'.'.join(irc.client.VERSION)
```

which raises the Type Error since it can't join said tuple of integers and instead expects a tuple of strings.

Easiest way to fix would be to change the above call to


```
#!python

'.'.join(map(str, irc.client.VERSION))
```

but I'm figuring you'll opt for a more readable fix and perhaps an accompanying test.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/jaraco/irc/issue/28
- Originally reported by: Nick Raptis
- Originally created at: 2013-09-04T22:14:12.659

issue_title: .pack() spoils the list in its argumets
issue_body:
gmpy 2.0.7
Python 3.4.2 Amd-64 (windows 7-64)
gmpy2.pack() spoils the list in its argumets. And, what dazzled me especially deeply, it also spoils some of the copies of original list. Not just named links to original list, but totally different copies with distinct names and content.

This error arises rarely. Consider the following code:

```
import gmpy2
from gmpy2 import mpz
import copy

def print_array(parNotes, parArray, parBase=None) :
	print(parNotes)
	if parBase == None : parBase = 16
	for r in range(len(parArray)) :
		if type(parArray[r]).__name__ == "mpq" :
			print("num=", hex(parArray[r].numerator).rjust(77))
			print("den=", hex(parArray[r].denominator).rjust(77))
			print()
		elif type(parArray[r]).__name__ == "mpz" :
			print(parArray[r].digits(parBase).rjust(77))
		else :
			print(hex(parArray[r]).rjust(77))

num1 = mpz(0x3be270e2e2cc865b9790e46f061972675da0d7d2c6edf0ce8277b93b)

dif = gmpy2.unpack(num1, 16)
dif_copy_list = list(dif) # distinct copy by list()
dif_copy_list[0] = 1 #to show that this is really another memory area/object tan original 'dif' list
dif_copy_slice = dif[:] # distinct copy by slice
dif_copy_slice[0] = 2 #to show that this is really another memory area/object tan original 'dif' list
dif_copy_copy = copy.copy(dif) # distinct copy by copy.copy()
dif_copy_copy[0] = 3 #to show that this is really another memory area/object tan original 'dif' list
dif_copy_deepcopy = copy.deepcopy(dif) # distinct copy by copy.copy()

print_array("dif", dif)
print_array("dif_copy_list", dif_copy_list)
print_array("dif_copy_slice", dif_copy_slice)
print_array("dif_copy_copy", dif_copy_copy)
print_array("dif_copy_deepcopy", dif_copy_deepcopy)
print("we have 5 different memory areas/lists with different names")

print("now spoiling just 1 original array 'dif' by packing into num2")
num2 = gmpy2.pack(dif, 16)
print("num1==num2", num1==num2)

print_array("dif", dif)
print_array("dif_copy_list", dif_copy_list)
print_array("dif_copy_slice", dif_copy_slice)
print_array("dif_copy_copy", dif_copy_copy)
print_array("dif_copy_deepcopy", dif_copy_deepcopy)

print("And now all the distinct memory areas - differently named copies of original list - ARE SPOILED ALSO, except the copy.deepcopy()")

```

issue_title: Support for IPv6 extension headers?
issue_body:
_From [o.a.stephens](https://code.google.com/u/o.a.stephens/) on March 02, 2010 13:29:04_

From examining the SVN checkout, there appears to be no support for IPv6 
extension headers within dpkt.

The reason I ask is that it isn't currently possibly to guarantee the 
ability to detect the payload protocol of an IPv6 packet. 
In the instance that there are no extension headers, then the 'nxt' header 
attribute is set to the payload protocol. However, if there are any 
extension headers there is no way to follow the header chain to find the 
payload protocol (or if desired, decode the extension headers).

Is there any code floating around to provide this support, or is it the 
case that it simply hasn't been implemented in dpkt?

Thanks

_Original issue: http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=31_

issue_title: traitlets declarations should use Integer instead of Int to support Python 2.7 (win)
issue_body:
nglview:             0.6.2.2-py27_1           omnia
ipywidgets:          5.2.2-py27_1                      
traitlets:           4.3.2-py27_0                  

Python 2.7 distinguishes between long and int. The traitlets library handles this case automatically by the Integer class (rather than Int), eg. https://traitlets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/trait_types.html?highlight=long#traitlets.Integer

Currently the Widget class uses Int, which leads to the following error on Python-2.7 (windows): https://ci.appveyor.com/project/gph82/molpx/build/1.0.38/job/45vglurnjx53oy8p#L1121

issue_title: Please document that this is a wrapper for the CLI svn client

Suggest handling resource absence like in e.g. doLOCK.

issue_title: Short names according to RFC4512 section 6.2 not working as expected
issue_body:
According to [RFC4512 section 6.2](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4512#section-6.2) I'd expect short names of attributes to point to the same property of an ldap3.abstract.entry.Entry. This is however not the case.

This becomes frustrating when dealing with schema that have the same attribute as a MUST, but are referring to it using different aliases. One example of this is `account` and `inetOrgPerson`:

```python
>>> obj_def = ObjectDef(['account', 'inetOrgPerson'], conn)
>>> obj_def
OBJ : account, inetOrgPerson [account (Structural) 0.9.2342.19200300.100.4.5, top (Abstract) 2.5.6.0, inetOrgPerson (Structural) 2.16.840.1.113730.3.2.2, organizationalPerson (Structural) 2.5.6.7, person (Structural) 2.5.6.6, top (Abstract) 2.5.6.0]
MUST: cn, objectClass, sn, userid
MAY : audio, businessCategory, carLicense, departmentNumber, description, destinationIndicator, displayName, employeeNumber, employeeType, facsimileTelephoneNumber, givenName, homePhone, homePostalAddress, host, initials, internationaliSDNNumber, jpegPhoto, l, labeledURI, localityName, mail, manager, mobile, o, organizationName, organizationalUnitName, ou, pager, photo, physicalDeliveryOfficeName, postOfficeBox, postalAddress, postalCode, preferredDeliveryMethod, preferredLanguage, registeredAddress, roomNumber, secretary, seeAlso, st, street, telephoneNumber, teletexTerminalIdentifier, telexNumber, title, uid, userCertificate, userPKCS12, userPassword, userSMIMECertificate, x121Address, x500uniqueIdentifier
```
From this, I'd expect if I specify `uid`, `sn` and `cn`, I'd not be missing mandatory attributes.

However:
```python
>>> writer = Writer(conn, obj_def)
>>> entry = writer.new("uid=foo,ou=users,dc=my-domain,dc=com")
>>> entry.sn="Name"
>>> entry.cn="Some Name"
>>> entry
DN: uid=foo,ou=users,dc=my-domain,dc=com - STATUS: Virtual, Missing mandatory attributes - READ TIME: <never>
    cn: <Virtual>
        CHANGES: [('MODIFY_REPLACE', ['Some Name'])]
    objectClass: <Virtual>
                 CHANGES: [('MODIFY_REPLACE', ['account', 'inetOrgPerson'])]
    sn: <Virtual>
        CHANGES: [('MODIFY_REPLACE', ['Name'])]
    uid: <Virtual>
         CHANGES: [('MODIFY_REPLACE', ['foo'])]
>>> writer.commit()
[...]
LDAPCursorError: mandatory attributes 'userid' missing in entry uid=foo,ou=users,dc=my-domain,dc=com
```

Trying to fix this by explicitely specifying `userid` does not work either:
```
>>> entry.userid="foo"
>>> writer.commit()
[...]
LDAPAttributeOrValueExistsResult: LDAPAttributeOrValueExistsResult - 20 - attributeOrValueExists - None - attribute 'uid' provided more than once - addResponse - None
```

This makes using the abstraction layer for this ~rather common combination~ of objectClasses basically unusable.
*EDIT*: The example is a bit contrived, since it's actually two structural objectclasses. However, the problem is also evident when creating e.g. an `account` entry with RDN `uid=foo` instead of `userid=foo`:
```python
>>> obj_def = ObjectDef(['account'], conn)
>>> writer = Writer(conn, obj_def)
>>> entry = writer.new("uid=foo,ou=users,dc=my-domain,dc=com")
[...]
LDAPCursorError: rdn type 'uid' not in objectclass definition
```

issue_title: modify operations not preserved through referral
issue_body:
When connecting to an LDAP server that returns a referral on modify, only the last operation is performed for an attribute. The same behavior is not observed when connecting directly to the master server, where all operations are performed.

The connection object is setup the same in both cases, with only the "hostname" in one case being the master and in the other case a slave (which returns a referral to the master)

    serverLDAP = ldap3.Server(host = hostname, port = 636, 
        use_ssl = True, connect_timeout = 3, get_info=ldap3.ALL)
    conn = ldap3.Connection(serverLDAP, lazy = False, raise_exceptions = True,
        authentication = ldap3.SIMPLE, check_names=True, user=userDN, password=password)
    conn.bind()

I then modify an object's description field, which has been manually reset to ["foo"] after each attempt.

    conn.modify("cn=my_test_group,ou=Groups,dc=domain,dc=local",
        {'description': [
            (ldap3.MODIFY_ADD, ["bar"]),
            (ldap3.MODIFY_DELETE, ["foo"])
    ]})

The expected behavior is of course that the description field should contain ["bar"] afterwards.
When making the change directly on the LDAP master, the audit logs the change as expected:

    dn: cn=my_test_group,ou=Groups,dc=domain,dc=local
    changetype: modify
    add: description
    description: bar
    -
    delete: description
    description: foo

But when making the change on the slave and following the referral the master audit logs the following:

    dn: cn=my_test_group,ou=Groups,dc=domain,dc=local
    changetype: modify
    delete: description
    description: foo

If I reverse the order of the two operations or add additional operations on the same object, it's still only the last operation that is actually performed.
The same behavior is seen when the attribute has multiple values before the modification.
If I attempt to modify two attributes at the same time (with more than one operation each) then only the last operation in each of the attributes is performed.

The test was done towards OpenLDAP/slapd v2.4.44, using python 3.5.0 and 3.6.1 with ldap3 v2.2.4.

issue_title: Database creation may be unnecessarily slow due to missing indexes
issue_body:
gffutils database creation may be unnecessarily slow since some sqlite indexes that are important for duplicate merging seem to be missing. The problematic part where the code spends most of the time is in [` _candidate_merges()`](https://github.com/daler/gffutils/blob/master/gffutils/create.py#L336). The joins in this function are expensive since apparently the `newid` and `idspecid` indexes are missing. I added:

```python
        c.execute('CREATE UNIQUE INDEX featuresid ON features (id);')
        c.execute('CREATE UNIQUE INDEX dupnewid ON duplicates (newid);')
        c.execute('CREATE INDEX dupspecid ON duplicates (idspecid);')
        self.conn.commit()
```

[below the other index preparation steps](https://github.com/daler/gffutils/blob/master/gffutils/create.py#L881) which resulted in an about 100-fold faster database creation using these indexes.

As a side node, I also changed the default pragmas to increase performance a little - but I believe the main boost came from the indexes alone:

```python
        pragmas={'synchronous':'OFF', 'journal_mode': 'MEMORY', 'main.page_size': 4096,\
                 'main.cache_size': 100000, 'temp_store': 2, 'locking_mode': 'EXCLUSIVE'}
```

issue_title: bulkCmd() returning varbind not in scope of requested OID when maxRepetitions == (numSubElements + 1)
issue_body:
we recently switched from easysnmp to pysnmp just because of the excellent MIB services. I'm quite happy with pysnmp, although we needed to fix at least 3 to 4 issues in various Cisco MIBs. But anyway, using the hlapi in conjunction with the MIB sevices is a great pleasure!

Having said that we came across an issue in a very particular situation:
Calling cmdgen.CommandGenerator().bulkCmd(...) with maxRepititions set to a number that is exactly one above the number of elements to be returned from the last SNMP GETBULK response will return exaxtly one varbind to much. The surplus varbind contains the next OID not in the scope of the requested OID with an empty value. So, if you only check for the last element in the returned OID (the index) and not the whole OID you will most likely get an index of 1 and thus clear your first result when copying out the results.

Example: if you do a bulkCmd()  for "IF-MIB::ifDescr" with maxRepititions set to 10 (or 5) and it happens that you should get 9 varbinds (interface descriptions) you will get 10 varbinds, the last being not in the scope of "IF-MIB::ifDescr". 

Also, I observed in Wireshark that in this particular situation bulkCmd() will do one SNMP GETBULK too much.

I'm _attaching a python script to check this issue:

[getIfDescrs.py.txt](https://github.com/etingof/pysnmp/files/2200920/getIfDescrs.py.txt)

I could reproduce the issue on various Cisco device types and different OIDs. Here's the output of the test script for a device having 9 interfaces with results being OK ( numRepetitions = 4 or 8) and being not OK ( numRepetitions = 5 or 10):

issue_title: scp context and return code
issue_body:
It looks like the SCPClient context accepts a transport internally and then executes on a channel, but it doesn't appear to provide a mechanism for accessing the channel's return code.

```
    with scp.SCPClient(transport) as scp_transport:
        scp_transport.put(localpath, remotepath)
```

With the above code, the `scp_transport.channel` is `None` after `put` is executed and `scp_transport` does not contain the return code.

Proposal here is to capture return code information prior to running a `self.close` at the end of `put`.  New code is below:

```
class SCPClient(object):
    """
    An scp1 implementation, compatible with openssh scp.
    Raises SCPException for all transport related errors. Local filesystem
    and OS errors pass through.
    Main public methods are .put and .get
    The get method is controlled by the remote scp instance, and behaves
    accordingly. This means that symlinks are resolved, and the transfer is
    halted after too many levels of symlinks are detected.
    The put method uses os.walk for recursion, and sends files accordingly.
    Since scp doesn't support symlinks, we send file symlinks as the file
    (matching scp behaviour), but we make no attempt at symlinked directories.
    """
    def __init__(self, transport, buff_size=16384, socket_timeout=5.0,
                 progress=None, sanitize=_sh_quote):
        """
        Create an scp1 client.
        @param transport: an existing paramiko L{Transport}
        @type transport: L{Transport}
        @param buff_size: size of the scp send buffer.
        @type buff_size: int
        @param socket_timeout: channel socket timeout in seconds
        @type socket_timeout: float
        @param progress: callback - called with (filename, size, sent) during
            transfers
        @param sanitize: function - called with filename, should return
            safe or escaped string.  Uses _sh_quote by default.
        @type progress: function(string, int, int)
        """
        self.transport = transport
        self.buff_size = buff_size
        self.socket_timeout = socket_timeout
        self.channel = None
        self.preserve_times = False
        self._progress = progress
        self._recv_dir = b''
        self._rename = False
        self._utime = None
        self.sanitize = sanitize
        self._dirtimes = {}
        self.return_code
```

```
    def close(self):
        """close scp channel"""
        if self.channel is not None:
            self.return_code = self.channel.exit_status
            self.channel.close()
            self.channel = None
```

issue_title: Retracting and asserting within count(n) loop
issue_body:
When using `count(n)` and the `for f in m` loop, trying to retract, change a fact value, and then re-assert doesn't seem to work as expected.

The value of the fact looks like so:

```ruby
puts f.box.inspect
#<Engine::Content:0x007fc77ea72310 @_d={"box_id"=>"B1", "$f"=>1, "position"=>"A1", "type"=>"xyz", "id"=>"$0232510393253996315", "t"=>"box", "count"=>2, "sid"=>"1509915080"}>
```

The flow here is like so:

```ruby
 ... when_all(count(4) ...

for f in m do
  retract(f.box)
  f.box.count += 1
  assert(f.box)
end
```

It seems that instead of replacing the existing fact, it creates a new/second one. Is it possible the `$f`, `id`, and/or `sid` keys are not correctly matching against the existing fact during the retract process?

[MSH file format version 4](http://gmsh.info/doc/texinfo/gmsh.html#MSH-file-format-_0028version-4_0029) is generated by default in Gmsh 4, which is now the current stable release, but cannot be read with `meshio.read` which asserts that the MSH format is version 2. 

To reproduce, get say Gmsh's [tutorial/t1.geo](https://gitlab.onelab.info/gmsh/gmsh/blob/master/tutorial/t1.geo) and mesh it
```shell
gmsh -2 t1.geo
```
to produce t1.msh (attached here gzipped as [t1.msh.gz](https://github.com/nschloe/meshio/files/2322131/t1.msh.gz)).  Then
```shell
python -c 'import meshio; meshio.read("t1.msh")'
```
raises
```

issue_title: Support for W292
issue_body:
W292: no newline at end of file

autopep8 actual behavior:
```bash
$ printf 'example' | autopep8 -
example
```
actual pycodestyle behavior:
```bash
$ printf 'example' | pep8 -
stdin:1:8: W292 no newline at end of file
```
correct example:
```bash
$ printf 'example\n' | pep8 -
```

Why: Per [POSIX definition](http://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/basedefs/V1_chap03.html#tag_03_206) a line of a text file need to end with a newline. Even if PEP8 does not require, most IDEs forces a new line at the end of file. If autopep8 does not, you always have unnecessary diff noise.

issue_title: Do not increase _connections_count in case of connect error
issue_body:
In case of connection error _connections_count is inscreased and _used_connections is appended by new connection. But, instead of returning this connection we got OperationalError. From now on there is no way to close this connection.
After _max_connections tries there is no place for new connections, so we got pool, full of dead connections, with no chance to fix them, because all of them are in _used_connections.
I suppose you could solve it by fixing one line:

```
    connection = Connection(self, *self._args, **self._kwargs)
+   connection_future = connection.connect()
    connection.set_close_callback(self.connection_close_callback)
    self._connections_count += 1
    self._used_connections[id(connection)] = connection
-   connection_future = connection.connect()
    IOLoop.current().add_future(connection_future, on_connected)
```

issue_title: Add to documentation that adjust_text() needs to be called last!
issue_body:
Hey there,

It's a very very useful library, but I banged my head so many times trying to use it that I find I unfortunate that it could have saved so many hours if the documentation was a bit more guiding, and I guess most users either lost a lot of time like me or just dropped the library in frustration.

I feel the documentation should be updated to at least include:
1. the function signature (name of function, arguments, docstring -- the latter will be a quite convenient replacement for a documentation until a proper one is redacted)
2. a simple example (just something basic like your first example -- if possible outside of a function to avoid confusing new users as much as possible, unnecessary constructs should be kept at a minimum for a minimal quickstart example)
3. add an important note: **For consistent behavior and best display results, adjust_text() should be called last, after drawing the whole figure, the labels and resizing. To do that, simply store all text objects in a list `texts`, and call `adjust_text(texts)` just before `plt.show()`**.

I think this last note is the most important of all additions to the readme, as calling adjust_text() anytime before is bound to produce undefined behavior, which is not specified in a clear manner anywhere.

Indeed, even if one calls `adjust_text()` only on a subset of text objects, it will still produce erratic behavior, like text moved far away from points (I could not pinpoint the root cause), or outside the boundary of the image (the latter only if the image gets resized afterward).

I am looking to implement the usage of the TLS Extension for Certificate Transparency. 
From openssl itself it can be queried like that:

### Observation
After using the `IPRoute` instance to setup networking as my application needs, I then attempt to use `IPDB` to monitor for changes that I must react to.  When starting my instance, I'm getting a repeated failure:

```
2016-05-31 09:07:21 - pyroute2.ipdb.main - ERROR - Restarting IPDB instance after error:

  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyroute2/ipdb/main.py", line 1052, in serve_forever

```

This failure continues at a frequency of 2-4 incidents per second.  When this occurred, my kernel also hit an OOM condition in the 3rd second of operation of my daemon to which my daemon is to blame.  I suspect that the above failure is significantly contributing to the memory exhaustion.

### Setup

- **Kernel:** 3.14.0-rc2

issue_title: COT shouldn't clone existing items to lower InstanceID
issue_body:
Because COT always picks the lowest unused InstanceID when creating an Item, when given an OVF with non-consecutive InstanceIDs (e.g., 1, 2, 3, 4, 10, 11, 12, 2001, 2002) then cloning existing Items will result in them being out-of-order (e.g., cloning Item 2002 will result in a new Item with InstanceID 5), which can subsequently cause problems because COT iterates over items in InstanceID order (so the newly cloned Item comes before the item it was cloned from, rather than after as COT expects).

Probably COT should always clone to an InstanceID higher than the item being cloned.

After a bit of research in the old Sulley code I found a try/except pair that is not present in the boofuzz code. Testing with the following change in `boofuzz/boofuzz/utils/debugger_thread_pydbg.py`fixes the problem:

```
def pre_send(self):

```

issue_title: Test for Google CA Store may not be what sslyze intended
issue_body:
I tested out `sslyze` on a server I help administer which uses a certificate from Let's Encrypt, and it was registered as trusted by every CA store except for Google's:

```
  * Certificate - Trust:
      Hostname Validation:               OK - Subject Alternative Name matches 18f.gsa.gov
      Mozilla NSS CA Store (02/2016):    OK - Certificate is trusted
      Microsoft CA Store (02/2016):      OK - Certificate is trusted
      Java 6 CA Store (Update 65):       OK - Certificate is trusted
      Apple CA Store (OS X 10.11.3):     OK - Certificate is trusted
      Google CA Store (02/2016):         FAILED - Certificate is NOT Trusted: unable to get local issuer certificate
```

I dug into `catt` and [it looks like](https://github.com/nabla-c0d3/catt/blob/master/sslyze.md#google) you're pulling from https://pki.google.com/roots.pem for the `Google CA Store`.

On the [Google PKI FAQ](https://pki.google.com/faq.html), this root file is referenced as a store of certificates to use when connecting to _Google services_:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4592/13211728/0923c6ac-d909-11e5-86b8-d2a7865f4334.png)

In other words, unlike the other root stores used by `sslyze`, this root store is not relevant to determining whether a certificate is trusted by a particular platform. This explains why it does not include Identrust, Let's Encrypt's cross-signer, yet Let's Encrypt certs are accepted just fine on Chrome and Android.

My understanding of Google's [root certificate program policies](https://www.chromium.org/Home/chromium-security/root-ca-policy#TOC-Root-Certificate-Programs) is that Chrome typically defers to the underlying OS for a root store. 

For Android, they link to their bug tracker, and some searching around shows that they [generally](https://code.google.com/p/android/issues/detail?id=59811&can=1&q=root%20certificate&colspec=ID%20Status%20Priority%20Owner%20Summary%20Stars%20Reporter%20Opened) make Mozilla review [a prerequisite](https://code.google.com/p/android/issues/detail?id=60439&can=1&q=root%20certificate&colspec=ID%20Status%20Priority%20Owner%20Summary%20Stars%20Reporter%20Opened), and then follow [undisclosed internal policies and private discretion](https://code.google.com/p/android/issues/detail?id=57624&can=1&q=root%20certificate&colspec=ID%20Status%20Priority%20Owner%20Summary%20Stars%20Reporter%20Opened) on whether to accept a CA.

[This 2013 comment](https://code.google.com/p/android/issues/detail?id=57624#c3) has a Google team member placing the AOSP certificate store at `libcore/luni/src/main/files/cacerts`. However, when I checked out [libcore from source](https://android.googlesource.com/platform/libcore/), the `libcore/luni/src/main` directory didn't have a `files/` directory.

Going to [libcore2](https://android.googlesource.com/platform/libcore2/) did have this directory, and it listed the following filenames:

```
$ ls
00673b5b.0  2fa87019.0  594f1775.0  81b9768f.0  aeb67534.0  ddc328ff.0
03e16f6c.0  2fb1850a.0  5a3f0ff8.0  8470719d.0  b0f3e76e.0  e48193cf.0

issue_title: IntegrityError response_ms
issue_body:
Hello all,

I just want to share an issue I had.

There was an exception regarding negative response time. After reading the code a bit I see that you have a justified assumption regarding the [`response_ms` being positive](https://github.com/aschn/drf-tracking/blob/master/rest_framework_tracking/models.py#L18).

The implementation of the duration is straightforward so no surprises there. My solution was to patch the [duration calculation](https://github.com/aschn/drf-tracking/blob/master/rest_framework_tracking/mixins.py#L88) to return 0 if the delta is negative.

Something like this:
`response_timedelta = now() - self.request.log.requested_at`
`response_ms = int(response_timedelta.total_seconds() * 1000)`
`response_ms = max(response_ms, 0)`

I wasn't able to reproduce the issue though.
I was wondering if anyone else have seen something similar.

Thanks

issue_title: Allow some command line options to be merged with those in the config
issue_body:
Taking from the first point of Features section of the readme:

> If a value is specified in more than one way then: command line > environment variables > config file values > defaults.

ConfigArgParse (CAP) overrides the values specified in the config file with those specified in the command line arguments. For most of the cases this behaviour is nice, except when it isn't.

Consider the case of a [mitmproxy user](https://github.com/mitmproxy/mitmproxy/issues/953) who has specified some scripts in the config file, and when they specify another script in the command line, the config ones get replaced.

The expected behaviour here is the merging of the list of scripts. 

I propose that CAP should add a new parameter (say `multiple`) to `parser.add_argument` that determines this override behaviour. So, for eg, the following snippet would result in an option that merges the values:

``` python
parser.add_argument(
    "-s", "--script", 
    action="append", type=str, dest="scripts" default=[],
    multiple="merge"
)
```

The `multiple` parameter could have values: 
- `merge` - Merge the values specified in the command line, env variables & config files & defaults.
- `override` - Follow the normal override process we follow now.

issue_title: Bug when join Augmentor to torchvision
issue_body:
Hi, everyone:
I just found a bug when I wanted to use `Augmentor.Pipeline` in `torchvision`. The following demo code can illustrate this:
```python
import torchvision
from torch.utils.data import Dataset
from PIL import Image

p = Augmentor.Pipeline()
p.rotate(probability=1.0, max_left_rotation=10, max_right_rotation=10)
p.zoom(probability=0.5, min_factor=1.1, max_factor=1.5)

transforms = torchvision.transforms.Compose([
    p.torch_transform(),
    torchvision.transforms.ToTensor(),
])

class MyDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, filenames, transform=None):
        """
        Args:
            filenames: list of file names.
        """
        self.filenames = filenames
        self.transform = transform

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        image = Image.open(self.filenames[idx])
        if self.transform:
            t_image = self.transform(image)
            return {'image':image, 'transformed_image':t_image}
        else:
            return {'image':image}


dataset = MyDataset(['./ISIC_0000000.jpg'], transform=transforms)
print(dataset[0])
```
Run this code directly could raise error `AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'size'`, this is because `Pipeline.torch_transform()` turns image into `list`, but `torch` need `PIL Image`. 

I just change one line in `Pipeline.py` file:
```python
         def _transform(image):
 560             for operation in self.operations:
 561                 r = round(random.uniform(0, 1), 1)
 562                 if r <= operation.probability:
 563                     image = [image]
 564                     image = operation.perform_operation(image)
 565                     image = image[0]   # --> changed this line
 566 
 567             return image
 568 
 569         return _transform

```

and it works good in my fork.

Is this a bug fixing to this project?

issue_title: Consider removing 4.0.22 from PyPI
issue_body:
Testing for the Stack Overflow question

[“Invalid character value for cast specification” when using pyodbc in Python](https://stackoverflow.com/q/49571954/2144390) 

reveals that it is reproducible with 4.0.22 under Python2. The same code works properly using 4.0.21.

If it looks like 4.0.23 might take a while to arrive then you may want to consider removing 4.0.22 from PyPI; instructions [here](https://stackoverflow.com/a/43603029/2144390). The issues with 4.0.22 are causing significant inconvenience for a lot of people and are damaging the pyodbc "brand".

issue_title: egStore remembers cached-like location of Settings file and refuses valid file name input
issue_body:
My application provides a path in which the Settings file should be created. Say, it was built on Machine A, in my case (Win 7) with path D:\Trials\Trial2\filenamehere.txt. 

I copied my application source to another machine, say Machine B ( in my case Win XP) and ran the python code. I supplied a valid filename and path to create the settings file, say, (E:\Temp\somefilenamehere.txt) . 

As suggested in the easyGUI tutorial on the easyGUI page, I created an inherited class based on EgStore, called Settings. 

However, when valid file name is provided and the store method is invoked, the Settings / EgStore class, still look for the settings file in the path in D:\ i.e. the path in the machine in which the code originally ran. 

When I tried to run the code from B, from my application's folder, where the D:\ path doesn't exist, it gives an error, saying FileNotFound (Errno 2). 
When I tried a minimal version of settings storage in the same folder, it didn't work.
The minimal version of settings storage in another folder worked. 

davidism at stack overflow, suggested I put an additional method in my subclass called restore, that reads thus :


def restore(self):
		filename = self.filename
		EgStore.restore(self)
		self.filename = filename		

This fixed it. 
An example program where I have used it is in Line No.128  of  ModiBatudu. 
https://github.com/whirlmind/ModiBatudu/blob/master/ModiBatudu_Python_Source/ModiBatudu.py

How the same code that uses the application path runs correctly in one folder and incorrectly in another folder is seen in this screenshot. Notice the D:\Trials.... etc in the error message. Where did that come from ? (Hint : From the path on the other machine where EgStore was used before. )

![problem_screenshot](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/12241348/7891727/92f367d6-066c-11e5-867b-350a723b0779.JPG)


The sample file called in that screenshot, from two different locations producing two different results is here : 

from easygui import *
import pprint
import os

#This class partially copy-pasted from easygui library demo. 
class Settings(EgStore):
	def __init__(self, filename):  # filename is required
		#-------------------------------------------------
		# Specify default/initial values for variables that
		# this particular application wants to remember.
		#-------------------------------------------------
		print("Test printing the path inside the Settings class");
		pprint.pprint(settingsFile);
		pprint.pprint("Exists ? " + str(os.path.isfile(settingsFile)));
		
		self.Last_Alert_TimeStamp_Num = ""

		#-------------------------------------------------
		# For subclasses of EgStore, these must be
		# the last two statements in  __init__
		#-------------------------------------------------
		self.filename = filename  # this is required
		self.restore()            # restore values from the storage file if possible

#-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------		

settingsFile = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "ModiBatudu_LastAlertTime.txt")	   

print("Test printing the path before calling the Settings class");
pprint.pprint(settingsFile);
pprint.pprint("Exists ? " + str(os.path.isfile(settingsFile)));

#Likely Problem Area begins ?......
settings = Settings(settingsFile)			
settings.Last_Alert_TimeStamp_Num = "Some junk goes here";	
settings.store()

issue_title: Does not work on WSL (Windows 10) - Suggestions Included
issue_body:
Unfortunately `catt` does not work on WSL on Windows 10.

The issue is in `python-zeroconf`, a library used by `pychromecast`, which is used by catt.

If you don't want to wait for the fix on `python-zeroconf` to be merged, `pychormecast` to be updated to use the fixed `python-zeroconf` and `catt` to be updated to use the new version of `pychomecast`, you can simpley type 

`pip install git+https://github.com/marcosdiez/pyzeroconf.git@now_it_works_on_wsl --upgrade`

I suggest this issue to be closed whenever all these merges occur.

issue_title: Why singleton scope doesn't work as expected for create_child_injector
issue_body:
Hi, 

I have a use case to take in user inputs (name, description) at runtime at a top-level class, while some lower-level class also needs to depend on user inputs. 

While I tried with the below code, it throws Exception: Instantiation of <class 'injector.description'> prohibited - it is derived from BaseKey so most likely you should bind it to something.

But if I remove the @singleton annotation for class B, it works. Can someone explain to me why singleton scope doesn't work in the child_injector? 

class base:
    def __init__(self, name):
        def configure(binder):
            binder.bind(Name, to=InstanceProvider(name))

        self.injector = Injector(configure)
        print('base: {}'.format(self.injector.get(Name)))


class A(base):
    def __init__(self, name, description):
        super(A, self).__init__(name)

        def configure(binder):
            binder.bind(Description, to=InstanceProvider(description))

        self.injector = self.injector.create_child_injector(configure)

        print(self.injector.get(B))


@singleton
class B:

    def __init__(self, name, description):

issue_title: BadWindow X11 error when closing print dialog on Linux
issue_body:
This is the error:

```
The program 'python' received an X Window System error.
This probably reflects a bug in the program.
The error was 'BadWindow (invalid Window parameter)'.
  (Details: serial 1064 error_code 3 request_code 3 minor_code 0)
  (Note to programmers: normally, X errors are reported asynchronously;
   that is, you will receive the error a while after causing it.
   To debug your program, run it with the --sync command line
   option to change this behavior. You can then get a meaningful
   backtrace from your debugger if you break on the gdk_x_error() function.)
```

Reproducible in hello_world.py, tkinter_.py and some other examples. It happens when setting `parent=NULL` in print_handler_gtk.cpp, but also occurs when setting `parent=CefBrowser_GetGtkWindow(browser_)`.

When clicking "Print" button in the dialog then everything works fine, there is no error. The error occurs only when clicking "X" close button in the print dialog.

In cefclient when closing print dialog there is also X window error:

```
[0611/191953.156656:WARNING:x11_util.cc(1362)] X error received: serial 9030, error_code 181 (GLXBadWindow), request_code 154, minor_code 32 (Unknown)
[0611/191953.158543:WARNING:x11_util.cc(1362)] X error received: serial 9032, error_code 9 (BadDrawable), request_code 153, minor_code 4 (Unknown)
[0611/191953.158695:WARNING:x11_util.cc(1362)] X error received: serial 9033, error_code 3 (BadWindow), request_code 4, minor_code 0 (Unknown)
```

However in cefclient it doesn't result in app termination. It seems that to resolve the issue it is required to install x11 error handlers that will ignore the badwindow error.

issue_title: Set AppUserModelID in subprocesses when "--app-user-model-id" switch is provided (pinning to taskbar issues on Windows 10)
issue_body:
On Windows 10 there are issues with pinning app to taskbar, sometimes duplicate app icons appear on taskbar or sometimes updating taskbar icon doesn't work. On Windows 10 applications are required to set AppUserModelID (AppID) string that uniquely identifies application on taskbar and allows to group app's seperate windows under a single taskbar button [1].  If application uses multiple processes it should set AppID for each of these processes (especially UI processes that create windows). A win32 function for that is `SetCurrentProcessExplicitAppUserModelID` [2]. 

A Python application can make such in the Python/Browser process using such code:
```
import sys
if 'win32' in sys.platform:
    import ctypes
    from ctypes.wintypes import HRESULT
    PCWSTR = ctypes.c_wchar_p
    AppUserModelID = ctypes.windll.shell32.SetCurrentProcessExplicitAppUserModelID
    AppUserModelID.argtypes = [PCWSTR]
    AppUserModelID.restype = HRESULT
    # An identifier that is globally unique for all apps running on Windows
    app_id = "change_this_to_some_unique_id"
    hresult = AppUserModelID(app_id)
    assert hresult == 0, "SetCurrentProcessExplicitAppUserModelID failed"
```

But that seems not enough. MSDN documentation [1] states that all app processes should set AppID. It seems that it is necessary to set it also for the Browser and Renderer processes in Chromium, as both of these processes create win32 windows. These two pull requests in the Electron and Atom project confirm that [3] [4].

The solution would be to pass the `--app-user-model-id` flag as a switch to `cef.Initialize()`. First this flag would need to be propagated from the Browser process to other processes and I think that passing switch to `cef.Initialize()` is enough to do that. Then the subprocess C++ code would need to check if this flag was passed and call `SetCurrentProcessExplicitAppUserModelID` [2] in that case, probably somewhere in `src/subprocess/cefpython_app.cpp`.

Ref:
[1] https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/dd378459(v=vs.85).aspx
[2] https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/dd378422(v=vs.85).aspx
[3] https://github.com/electron/electron/pull/2175
[4] https://github.com/atom/atom/pull/8170

issue_title: git.py should not require a version to clone repo
issue_body: Git supports the notion of cloning a repository without specifying a branch. While vcstool allows the 'version' to be optional, git.py requires a version minatorily. This requires my vcstool import yaml file to specify a version/branch. It seems one should be able to clone the default branch from the source git repository. This way the owner of the remote repository can set the default branch.

issue_title: Messages without a message_type appear as unnamed tasks
issue_body:
`Message`s without a `message_type` appear as `<UNNAMED TASK>`s.

So the following code:
```python
import eliot
import sys

eliot.to_file(sys.stdout)

with eliot.start_action(action_type=u"task"):
    eliot.Message.log(key=u"value")
```

Produces the following output:

```
7d074742-0116-4a57-a062-afa05bf13d5d
+-- task@1/started
    `-- timestamp: 2017-02-15 20:14:00.742261
    +-- <UNNAMED TASK>
        |-- key: value
        `-- timestamp: 2017-02-15 20:14:00.742498
    +-- task@3/succeeded
        `-- timestamp: 2017-02-15 20:14:00.742647
```

The message appears as an `<UNNAMED TASK>`.  Providing a `message_type` field results in the message appearing as a message.  This should be fixed or at least documented.

issue_title: Allow continuation on nargs=argparse.REMAINDER
issue_body:
This is somewhere between a feature and a possible bug due to inconsistency with argparse; bear with me.
Essentially I believe the argcomplete process should drop out without exiting if we fall into the scope of an `argparse.REMAINDER`. Right now it doesn't understand that with a REMAINDER argument specified, any options after the last positional argument wouldn't be interpreted by argparse. Since argparse won't interpret them, there's no sense trying to suggest autocompletions - right now I'm getting option suggestions which won't work.

This is important for my use-case as I'm trying to use argcomplete with a cli which is broken into multiple functions and works like this:

```python
        parser.add_argument(
            '--host', dest='host', type=str, default=None,
            help='The delivery server host'
        )
        parser.add_argument(
            '--port', dest='port', type=int, default=None,
            help='The delivery server port'
        )
       #...

        parser.add_argument(
            'command', nargs=1, choices=command_names,
            help='The command you wish to invoke'
        )
        parser.add_argument(
            'command_flags', nargs=argparse.REMAINDER, help=argparse.SUPPRESS
        )
        argcomplete.autocomplete(parser)

        args = parser.parse_args()

        Command = commands_by_name.get(args.command[0])

        if not Command:
            raise CommandError('Unknown command: %s' % args.command)

        return Command(
            host=args.host,
            port=args.port,
            url_prefix=args.prefix,
            logger=self.logger,
            args=args.command_flags
        )
```
Essentially I'm passing any remaining arguments after a command name to a `Command` class which then specifies its own secondary argparser, and I want to then run `argcomplete.autocomplete` again so that I can autocomplete the arguments to that subcommand.

I suspect this isn't actually too difficult to do, just needs a little bit of logic to check if the next argument would be interpreted as a `REMAINDER`, and a flag to allow dropping out at that point if desired (there are probably some cases where you could want to complete an indefinite number of arguments, if you were taking a file list or something, though it should never suggest options at this point). If I get a spare moment I'll see if I can implement it myself and create a pull request; I haven't looked at the codebase yet.

issue_title: Timeout on create_target()
issue_body:
I have a script that uses the IPTables package to modify my firewall rules. At one point, this code was working fine. Now, however, it times out on the call to create_target() hangs. No exception is thrown, the process just ends.

Here's my code:

```
table = iptc.Table(iptc.Table.FILTER)
chain = iptc.Chain(table, CHAIN_FORWARD)
clientRule = iptc.Rule()
clientRule.in_interface = TAP_IF
clientRule.src = clientIp
clientRule.dst = wpIp
clientRule.target = clientRule.create_target("ACCEPT")
if clientRule not in chain.rules:
    chain.append_rule(clientRule)
```

Any suggestions?
-Greg

issue_title: Global seeding does not work as expected
issue_body:
Consider the following code:
```python
import unittest
import random

from mimesis import Food

class Test(unittest.TestCase):
    def test_food(self):
        f = Food("en")
        print(">>", f.dish())

if __name__ == "__main__":
    random.seed("123")
    unittest.main()
```

I would expect that for consecutive runs the same `dish` is printed but it's not:

```bash
>> Macaroni and Cheese
.
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 0.001s

OK

Press ENTER or type command to continue
>> Ploye
.
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 0.001s

OK
```
The question now is whether that is expected behavior? I know there is a specific seed for each `Provider`.
Because I find it a bit annoying, requiring me to jump a few more hoops to get this under test.

```
>> python --version
Python 3.5.3
>> pip freeze|grep mime
mimesis==1.0.4
```

issue_title: Honcho export in Anaconda environment
issue_body:
Hi,
I tried to run honcho in an Anaconda environment and export the startup scripts for upstart.
At least running `honcho start` is possible by first activating the env:

```
source activate my_env && honcho start
```

However, exporting the init scripts does not work. It requires root permission but when I run `sudo honcho export -a myapp upstart /etc/init` it fails with `sudo: honcho: command not found` because for the root user there's no conda installation.

The only workaround I was able to do was to export the `*.conf` file as the user with conda env to a writable location, then move them to `/etc/init` and modify to add call to `source activate my_env`.

Can anyone imagine some more straightforward way? Thanks.

issue_title: tag white_list_pref with informational community
issue_body:
It would be good to tag announcements that were accepted _solely_ becaues of `white_list_pref` with a special community, so we can easily find those announcements and see where we need to help our participants.

`white_list_pref` prefixes should also have standard LP of 100

Ideally the special tag is not present on `white_list_pref` announcements if they were accepted because a valid IRR object came into existence.

issue_title: Negative temperatures when reading from Eurotherm3508
issue_body:
Morning.

I was using the ```eurotherm3500.py``` over the weekend to record temperatures from our oven as it was running an experiment.

Noticed in the logged data that negative numbers did not look like they were being converted correctly to a signed integer. For example the value -0.1 was being recorded as 6553.5

I added a small kludge to my code which sorted everything out:

    def eurotherm_sanitize_temp(temp):
        raw = int(temp*10)
        if raw > 32767:
            temp = -0.1*float(65536 - raw)
        return temp

Hope this note is of some use to you. You saved me a tonne of work by making this code available, so many thanks!

issue_title: "assume" in an always failing test gives hypothesis.errors.UnsatisfiedAssumption
issue_body:
So, I've been tinkering around with Hypothesis the last few days, and I've run into a fun one. In a couple of cases I've had a test that should always throw an exception because I've broken something in the code it's testing *and* has an `assume` statement at the beginning of the test function, I get a hypothesis.errors.UnsatisfiedAssumption not the expected Exception. Fixing the broken code makes the test pass, but I'd much rather have the exception to make my life easier when fixing the code...

I suspect a chunk of this is that I'm doing something a bit daft - testing a heavily stateful interface without use of `RuleBasedStateMachine` yet - and the `assume` is of the form "don't do a duplicate insert of an item with one of these ids that needs to be unique", so it may be failing due to trying to simplify something it can't run twice, but I'd still expect this to throw the Exception.

I'm using Hypothesis 1.18.1 from pypi on Python 3.5.0 on OS X

issue_title: Representation, Operation and Efficiency
issue_body:
Many of the objects used in the analysis are described mathematically as matrices.

A simplistic representation of a matrix in python is a list of lists:

`[[]]`

If this object is sparse then a lot of memory is allocated for empty entries. Furthermore, some operations require iteration over each element or each row/column of a matrix. If zero values do not contribute to the result of the operation then this time is wasted.

One way to represent the neighbour relation between source place pixels is with such a sparse matrix:

```
[[1, 1, 0, 0], 
 [1, 1, 1, 0],
 [0, 1, 1, 1], 
 [0, 0, 1, 1]]
```

In this representation a pixel address is a column or a row and a 1 indicates that two pixels are related. So pixel 0 has pixel 1 as a neighbour, pixel 1 has pixels 0 and 2 as a neighbour and so on.

This representation is very efficient for checking if two pixels are related. m[a, b] == 1 can be performed in constant time. However, if we wanted to perform some operation over all of the neighbours of pixel 0, for instance, we would have to check four entries in the matrix even though only two contain a non-zero value.

A more efficient representation is an list of lists of addresses:

`[[1], [0, 2], [1, 3], [2]]`

From sixteen elements we have reduced the size of the object to six. If we were to iterate over the neighbours of some pixel we could do that in O(N) time where N is the average number of neighbours.

If we wanted to check whether two pixels were neighbours that would now take O(N) time as well. We can recover a constant time lookup by using sets rather than lists:

`[{1}, {0, 2}, {1, 3}, {2}]`

These two objects still contain all the information that a simplistic matrix representation contained. We have all of the neighbour relationships and we can find the number of neighbours by checking the length of the list.

Another matrix representation of information in the analysis is the matrix that maps source pixels to image pixels:

```
[[0, 1, 0,    0], 
 [1, 0, 0.5, 0],
 [0, 0, 0.5, 0], 
 [0, 0, 0,    1]]
```

In this example, source pixel 0 maps to image pixel 1, source pixel 1 maps to image pixel 0 and so on. Source pixel 2 is an exception, as it maps half to source pixel 1 and half to source pixel 2.

This partial mapping means we can't use the representations used in the source-neighbour objects. 

An appropriate representation could be a list of dictionaries:

`[{1: 1}, {0: 1}, {1: 0.5, 2:0.5}, {3: 1}]`

Here the same information is given but only non-zero entries need be accounted for.

Now convolution acts on an individual dictionary.

Let's imagine a simple 3x3 kernel:

```
[[0, 0.2, 0],
 [0.2, 0.4, 0.2],
 [0, 0.2, 0]]
```

We take the dictionary for source pixel 1:

`{1: 1}`

In image space this looks something like:

```
[[0, 1],
 [0, 0]]
```

Applying the kernel would give:

```
[[0.2, 0.4],
 [0, 0.2]]
```

However, in our efficient regime we can neglect image space entirely and simply perform a transformation on our dictionary representation:

`{1: 1} -> {0: 0.2, 1: 0.4, 3: 0.2}`

Our f matrix is now formed of a list of convolved dictionaries.

This representation makes computation of each element in the F matrix far more efficient.

Take F01. Ignoring noise, this is the sum over j of f[0, j] * f[1, j]. 

Using the classic matrix representation of f we would have to check the entries in f[0] and f[1] for each j. That's O(I) time complexity where I is the number of image pixels.

Given the list of dictionary representation, we only need to check for keys that actually exist in the dictionary.

Let d0 be f[0] and d1 be f[1]. We iterate through the keys in d1 and if a key exists in d2 we take the values, multiple them and add them to the sum.
```

sum = 0
for image_pixel_index in d0.keys():
    if image_pixel_index in d1:
        sum += d0[image_pixel_index] * d1[image_pixel_index]
```

Checking a key is in a dictionary is performed in constant time. In effect, the number of operations performed is equal to the number of image pixels that source pixel 1 maps to. This means that calculation of F matrix operations using this scheme is vastly more efficient.

issue_title: *args deserve more explanations...
issue_body:
First of all, thanks for making such a great library. This literally SAVED my life. 
The idea of letting people take advantage of vectorizing and multi-threading on Pandas is just wonderful. 

However because this library is relatively new, I don't get much information from googling swifter.

This time I wanted to swiftapply values from 2 columns and I had no idea how to do this. 
I eventually figured out I should just repeat like below but it was hard (for me) to figure it out. 
```

def anyfunction(a,b,c):
    blablabla

df["New_Column"] = swiftapply(df["A"], anyfunction , b = df["B"], c = df["C"])

```

Moreover, it was even harder to get the clear picture of how these parameters look like when they are inside my function. 

First I tried to compare two values like

```
def anyfunction(a,b,c):
    if a > b:
        return foo

```
However, I later figured out this could not be done because they originally come in the form of pd.Series. I had no idea this would have been the case before a lot of debugs. 

Someone else can be confused too because while the above function doesn't work, the below works:

```
def anyfunction(a,b,c):
    return a + b

```

So the origin of confusion is that the arguments are always pd.Series in swiftapply but sometimes they behave like normal values and sometimes not. 
Sometimes they can be 'a', but other times they should be 'a[0]'. 

I think many people will be less confused if these things were somewhere in the document as examples. 


Anyway, again thanks for your great library. This is just beautiful.

issue_title: Feature request: Allow identifying formats by name
issue_body:
Today, when dealing with complex an multiple formats, I use the following peace of code to be able to retrieve them at the time to write data :

```python
def register_formats(wb, d):
    return {k: wb.add_format(v) if v is not None else v for k, v in d.items()}

#my_formats d parameter simplified for readability sake
my_formats = register_formats(workbook, {
        'error':  {'bg_color': '#cc0000'},
        'warning': {'bg_color': '#ffff00'},
        'ok': None})

# Much farther in the program ...

ws = wb.add_worksheet()
ws.write(0,0,"text", my_formats['error'])

```

but then one has to pass this my_format dictionary around.

I suggest to extend the workbook interface with an optional name parameter, and then in the write functions be able to give the name of the format rather than the format itself. IT would result in something like :
```python
wb.add_format({'bg_color': '#cc0000'}, ref="error")

#####

ws.write(0,0,"text", format_ref="error")

```

I can propose an implementation if that helps.

issue_title: Filtering doesn't seem to be working
issue_body:
It doesn't seem like the filters are being picked up properly

I tried a bunch of options based on (https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/FilterAndPatternSyntax.html)

And none of them produce anything. Here are examples

```
awslogs get cloudfront-logs/****--start='1h' --filter-pattern="{ $.errorCode = 400 }" 
awslogs get cloudfront-logs/**** --start='1h' --filter-pattern="[ip, user, username, timestamp, request, status_code = 4*, bytes]" 
```

Am I doing the filtering correctly?

issue_title: Add job from gunicorn worker, when scheduler runs in master-process
issue_body:

issue_title: Support other types of N-grams
issue_body:
The NGram class can already do non-character N-grams by providing pad_char=(None,) and then either adding tuples or writing a key function that return tuples.

Make this explicit by:
- renaming pad_char to padding
- renaming "string" parameters to "key" for example in _split, split, pad
- change mentions of "string" in doctstrings to read "key" instead
- docstring for "key" and "padding" should mention tuples are valid return type
- renaming "query" parameter to "key" in search, find, items_sharing_ngrams
- create "paditem" method to return padded key for items
- noting support for other types of N-grams (such as words or general sublists) in README
- adding doctest examples in tutorial.rst for word N-grams and number N-grams

issue_title: Strange problem with row matrices
issue_body:
Hi,
First I have been using eigency for a few months now and I absolutely love it! However, I see a repeated strange issue which occurs when I try to pass **row matrices** (that is, numpy arrays with a shape of `(1, N)`) from cython to c++ via Eigency.

When I pass a matrix of shape `(M, N)` the shape of the Eigen matrix is also `(M, N)` as it should be. But when I pass a matrix of shape `(1, N)` the Eigen matrix ends up transposed, having shape `(N, 1)`.

I'm aware of the common problem with non-fortran-order arrays appearing to be transposed once they reach C++, and as far as I can tell that's not what's happening here as it happens even if I create my arrays with `order='F'`

I've [attached](https://github.com/wouterboomsma/eigency/files/856389/Archive.zip) some reproducing code. On my machine I compiled this with the commands:

```
g++-6 -I /usr/local/include/eigen3 -c cpp_print_shape.cpp
python setup.py build_ext --inplace
```

Running `python test.py` then shows the problematic behaviour I've been seeing.

Please let me know if I'm doing something wrong here, or if this is a bug!

issue_title: Bug in SVN segment when navigating to an ignored folder inside a versioned folder
issue_body:
I have the following structure:

    .../Lumis_PortalJava
                  |- portal_install

The `Lumis_PortalJava` is versioned in SVN.
It's child `portal_install` is ignored. 
Whenever I enter in `portal_install` (and any subfolder), I get the following error:

![powerline-shell-svn-bug](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/10933706/39313969-a1856966-4949-11e8-8b1d-54134c3abf70.png)

It seems to be a problem with the following method: [_get_svn_revision()](https://github.com/b-ryan/powerline-shell/blob/73b01d659732637e72e4fe53fb430ccabc528d57/powerline_shell/segments/svn.py#L5).

Either this method should return an empty revision (what would produce a strange behavior, IMHO) or [parse_svn_stats](https://github.com/b-ryan/powerline-shell/blob/73b01d659732637e72e4fe53fb430ccabc528d57/powerline_shell/segments/svn.py#L17) should ignore ingored files or folders (those line starting with `I`). The second approach seems to produce a better user experience (but I haven't tried any of those to be sure).

issue_title: Make a final editorial pass through the PEP before posting it to python-dev
Handler for tokens recognition/disambiguation
issue_body:
parglare currently calls recognizers one by one and in case of multiple match do lexical [disambiguation strategy](http://www.igordejanovic.net/parglare/disambiguation/#lexical-ambiguities) on its own based on the priorities and the length of the match.

In same cases it would be better to register a handler that will receive all possible tokens expected at the current place and let the handler decide which of the tokens is the next in the input.

**Motivation:** there are use-cases where we want to do fuzzy matching of tokens. This approach gives us a chance to decide which token is the best match at the given location in case there are no exact matches. We even get a chance to dynamically decide which token is the right one in case of multiple matches.

Refactor QueueReader to use single async task
issue_body:
Currently the queue reader has next method:
```python
    def ready(self):
        assert not self.is_waiting, "ready() got called while waiting for a frame to be read"
        self.is_waiting = True

        t = asyncio.async(self._read_next(), loop=self._loop)
        if _TEST:  # this feels hacky to me
            t._log_destroy_pending = False
```
If can be rewritten to only contain 1 task, that will always wait for new data and push it to queue.

``` After we remove o suspend this part there is still "Mark subforums read" link left behind. This link comes from templates/machina/forum/forum_detail.html file. On the line 17 sub_forums returns a tree object. Even with an empty tree there still object exists and if statement always return True. Here we should add ".visible_forums" property to overcome this issue. ``` {% if sub_forums# .visible_forums %}
{% if request.user.is_authenticated %}
 {% trans "Mark subforums read" %}
{% endif %}
```

issue_title: update() is slow
issue_body:
My colleague tried to replace old progressbar with progressbar2 in django-cities-light (see this branch https://github.com/greenday2/django-cities-light/commits/feature/progressbar2) and noticed that data import became much slower (~2 hours instead of ~20 minutes). This also results in one CPU core being busy with iTerm only (probably related to frequent terminal redrawing) We worked around this issue by introducing `divisor` variable and less frequent calls to update().

I think it is worth informing you about the fact that there is serious performance degradation in your fork, so you may want either to fix the cause, or to introduce some laziness in update(), for example update the progressbar only if input variable has changed enough to draw the next character. You can use our divisor trick (and probably replace 100 with utils.get_terminal_size())

issue_title: Use pythonic sequence ducktyping instead of hardcoding to list type
issue_body:
The list of objects,and the update_fields and exclude_fields arguments break when passed a set, tuple, iterable or any other sequence type that is not specifically a list.

This is not very pythonic, most API's allow to pass whatever and will convert if their internals require a specific type of sequence.

This is annoying if we pass things like dict.keys() as update_fields, or a set of deduped objects etc.

issue_title: Wordlist parsing bug
issue_body:
The following triggered a bug:
`./crackmapexec 127.0.0.1 -u user -p pass_file`

Tool didn't manage to load passwords form a file (pass_file) which was in the current working directory. I have managed to pin point the issue (crackmapexec.py [line 162]):
`for user, passw, ntlm_hash in zip(args.username, args.password, args.hash):`

When `hash` command line argument is not supplied, it becomes an empty item within `zip` list (or whatever it creates). This makes the Python interpreter to bypass the subsequent code block (lines 163-173) which should read the file contents and load the list of usernames || passwords || hashes. The result is that the file name (in this case - pass_file) is treated as a standalone password.

Would be nice to see this fixed ;]

issue_title: Improvement: opsec auto-accept flag
issue_body:
## Steps to reproduce

1. Install CME v4.0.0.dev 'Sercurty'
2. Run CME v4.0.0.dev 'Sercurty' against multiple workstations using the module enum_chrome within a bash script.

## Command string used
`cme smb /path/to/ips.txt -M enum_chrome -d domain -u username -p password`

## CME verbose output (using the --verbose flag)
`[!] Module is not opsec safe, are you sure you want to run this? [Y/n]`

## OS
Kali Linux (latest as of post date)

## Target OS
Kali Linux (latest as of post date)

## Detailed issue explanation
I run CME in scripts that auto fill ips.txt, username, domain and password.  The enum_chrome module (among others) requires user input (Y or n) because it is not opsec safe.  Since this runs in a script, I can't seem to accept the message and the script will hang at this point.  Would it be possible to implement a flag to automatically accept non opsec-safe modules for within scripts?  Such as using a flag such as --opsec [y/n]:
`cme smb /path/to/ips.txt -M enum_chrome --opsec y -d domain -u username -p password`

This is a bit of a mix of a limitation with CME but also, of course, a limitation within my script.  Just wanted to point this out and I think it would be a helpful feature for some users.

!123blarblar123!

Version: 4.0.1dev

## OS
Darwin 17.5.0 Darwin Kernel Version 17.5.0

When supplying a password which contains a Bang character ! the system tries to recall last executed command from bash_history.  So in the case above it tried to execute the first command in bash_history and as such stops CME.

issue_title: your example is off
issue_body: You get the same model instance twice and save each and you get a concurrency error?  Why exactly?  You haven't changed any fields!  Optimistic concurrency means detecting changes to an object at save/commit that overwrite concurrent changes.  It generally requires keeping track of mutations of the object somehow or using a shadow object.  If this really works like the example it is broken by design.

issue_title: The infinite signal list bug
issue_body:
If I use only non-blocking acquires then nobody will consume signal list, but every process will push to it on release, so signal list will grow indefinitely.

And mixing blocking and non-blocking locks will end up in a total nightmare.

See #14 for related bug.

issue_title: Use JSON instead of pickle for tld data
issue_body:
.tld_set_snapshot and .tld_set use pickle to store the TLD information. While this is perfectly fine in most cases it brings the following issues:
 * Binary data stored in your git repository is a bad practice
 * If this library gets packaged for ubuntu/debian, the maintainer will complain. I just started using tldextract in the [w3af project](https://github.com/andresriancho/w3af/commit/72c09c57ed588caf9bc1b5c9de686b85ebe7c072) which is part of ubuntu/debian. When the package maintainer packages the next version he'll most likely dislike the binary blob
 * Pickles are "executables". A specially crafted pickle can trigger an arbitrary remote command execution when unpickled. While I did review the library for bugs and backdoors before including it in w3af, I did not read the whole pickle; which is bad for w3af user's security.

ExtractResult(subdomain='', domain='example', suffix='com')
>>> tldextract.extract('example..com')

>>> tldextract.extract('example...com')

Running this:
```python
redmine.project.create(name='Test Project',

Produces this:

issue_title: Logging in using user id instead of email address
issue_body:
It seems that it is possible to login with a user id rather than email address in the default setup. I have tested this by adding the following test to test_common.py
```
def test_authenticate_with_id(client):
    response = authenticate(client,email=1)
    assert response.status_code == 302
    response = authenticate(client,email=1, follow_redirects=True)
    assert b'Hello matt@lp.com' not in response.data
```

I think that to fix this behaviour class LoginForm in forms.py needs to set a validator on the email field:

`email = StringField(get_form_field_label('email'), [validators.Email()])`

issue_title: Part of the code for version 9.14 is not in the - pypi.python.org
Bind with logging in user first
issue_body:
This is a design question - is there any particularly practical reason as to why you [bind the user before you bind the LDAP Manager credentials](https://github.com/etianen/django-python3-ldap/blob/2a05d41a7de819d81d212d22ed85f716196677ec/django_python3_ldap/ldap.py#L147-L180)?  

I've typically done the inverse, where if manager credentials were specified, I would bind with the LDAP Manager credentials first and then validate the logging in users credentials second. This approach gives you the ability to:

- Rebind with secondary user credentials
- OR simply validate the user credentials which won't count against them in LDAP if they've typed their password incorrectly. 

I typically don't recommend option two, but some users may desire that functionality.

I cannot see any benefit to ever providing LDAP Manager credentials within the context laid out for django-python3-ldap.

Oh, and side note, but I'm really happy this project's around. It fulfills a lot of needs that have been lacking in this space. Great work!

django 1.11.7
python 3.6.3

I have a model with a field "value". It is a numeric(12,5) type.

Model.objects.get(value= ...) triggers an error for 8500.123123123 and 8500.1, but not for 85000.123123123, 8500.0.

django.db.utils.Error: ('HY104', '[HY104] [Microsoft][SQL Server Native Client 11.0]Invalid precision value (0) (SQLBindParameter)')

I have tried changing the ODBC driver to ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server, SQL Server Native Client 10, SQL Server Driver (ODBC SQL Server Driver) and had no change in results.

Here's my connection details:

```
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'sql_server.pyodbc',
        #'HOST' : '',
        #'USER' : '',
        #'PASSWORD' : '',
        'NAME' : 'Blarg',
        'OPTIONS' : {
            'dsn' : 'DataSource',
            'driver_supports_utf8' : True,
        }
    },
```

I interpreted the query Django thought was being executed and replicated it in a simple bit of code using only pyodbc:

```
import pyodbc # tested with 4.0.21

cnxn = pyodbc.connect('DSN=DataSource')
cursor = cnxn.cursor()

issue_title: Activation failed - email clients likely pre-fetching the activation URL?
issue_body:
Has anyone else run into email clients, specifically web based ones, pre-fetching the activation URL? This causes the User's email to be verified and therefore activated. When the User subsequently requests the activation URL, they are presented with a failure message, but their account has actually already been activated.

Given the design of setting the activation key to `ALREADY_ACTIVATED`, and passing the requested activation key back to the activation complete view upon failure, I don't see a straightforward way to distinguish between failures due to the User already being activated and every other failure scenario.

issue_title: Allow configuring dependencychecker, especially meta dependencies
issue_body:
What I mean is: in Plone packages you often do not import `Zope2`, but you import for example `Products.Five`.  But `Products.Five` is included in the `Zope2` package, at least for versions currently in use by Plone.

Let me give an example. For `collective.searchandreplace` I have updated the `setup.py` using dependency checker.  See https://github.com/collective/collective.searchandreplace/blob/663b9c5f7d44ce2f022bed18f4decd02f0a8ba4f/setup.py

Result:

```
Missing requirements
====================
     Products.Five.browser.pagetemplatefile.ViewPageTemplateFile

Missing test requirements
=========================
     Products.Five.fiveconfigure
     Products.Five.testbrowser.Browser
     Testing.ZopeTestCase
     Testing.ZopeTestCase.FunctionalDocFileSuite

Unneeded requirements
=====================
     Products.CMFPlone
     Zope2

```

Those missing requirements are already included in Zope2.  It would be nice if there was a way to tell this to dependencychecker.

Also, `Products.CMFPlone` is not itself imported, but I have added it because the package only makes sense in the context of a Plone site.

So I wonder if there is already something that handles this, or if the code can be improved to somehow realise that those packages are really part of a different package.

Otherwise I wonder if a config file can help.  Say a `~/.dependencychecker.cfg`:

```
[dependencychecker]
# Do not complain about these unneeded requirements:
unneeded_whitelist = Products.CMFPlone
# Maybe also:
# missing_whitelist = ...
# unneeded_test_whitelist = ...
# missing_test_whitelist = ...

[provides]
# Packages that provide (contain) other packages:
Zope2 =
    Products.Five
    Testing
```

issue_title: conn.open needs try/catch to retry
issue_body:
When sending the emails need to also try/catch the conn.open part so if the connection open fails it can retry all the messages.

```
...
    conn = get_connection(backend=settings.CELERY_EMAIL_BACKEND, **combined_kwargs)
    conn.open()

    messages_sent = 0

    for message in messages:
        try:
            sent = conn.send_messages([dict_to_email(message)])
            if sent is not None:
                messages_sent += sent
            logger.debug("Successfully sent email message to %r.", message['to'])
        except Exception as e:
            # Not expecting any specific kind of exception here because it
            # could be any number of things, depending on the backend
            logger.warning("Failed to send email message to %r, retrying. (%r)",
                           message['to'], e)
            send_emails.retry([[message], combined_kwargs], exc=e, throw=False)

    conn.close()
```

And if there is a SoftTimeLimitExceeded error, it should try cleanup and exit

issue_title: Please send a meaningful user-agent
issue_body: (Please correct me if I'm wrong, but) [AFAICS](https://github.com/mocnik-science/osm-python-tools/blob/master/OSMPythonTools/overpass.py#L41), you don't send any meaningful [user-agent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/User_agent) header when sending requests (to Overpass, Nominatim, …). Especially for these community-provided services it is very important to recieve such a header, in order to find out where traffic comes from and how the available resources can be distributed fairly.

issue_body:
I have following models and serializer the target is when serializer runs to have only one query:

Models:
```
class Assignee(EmbeddedDocument):
    id = ObjectIdField(primary_key=True)
    assignee_email = EmailField(required=True)
    assignee_first_name = StringField(required=True)
    assignee_last_name = StringField()
    assignee_time = DateTimeField(required=True, default=datetime.datetime.utcnow)
    user = ReferenceField('MongoUser', required=True)

class MongoUser(Document):

    password = StringField(required=True)
    first_name = StringField(required=True)
    last_name = StringField()
    assignees= EmbeddedDocumentListField(Assignee)
```

Serializers:

```

    class Meta:
        model = MongoUser
        fields = ('id', 'email', 'first_name', 'last_name', 'assignees')

class AssigneeSerializer(EmbeddedDocumentSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Assignee
        fields = ('assignee_first_name', 'assignee_last_name', 'user')
        depth = 0

```
When checking the mongo profiler I have 2 queries for MongoUser Document. If I remove the assignees field from MongoUserSerializer then there is only one query. 

As a workaround I've tried to use user_id field to store only ObjectId and changed AssigneeSerializer to:
```
class AssigneeSerializer(EmbeddedDocumentSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Assignee
        fields = ('assignee_first_name', 'assignee_last_name', 'user_id')
        depth = 0

```
But again there are 2 queries. I think that the serializer EmbeddedDocumentSerializer fetch all the fields and query for ReferenceField and 
```
fields = ('assignee_first_name', 'assignee_last_name', 'user_id') 
````
works after the queries are made.
How to use ReferenceField and do not run a separate query for each when serializing?

issue_title: Support use case with new obj creation at ReferenceField
issue_body:
Framework should support use case: "select an object from existent, or create new"

Serializer should support customized ReferenceField with behaviour:
* rendering as id or nested object as usual, according to depth value of serializer
* parsing as id or nested object, according to provided data
  * id is converted to DBRef or ObjectId, according to dbref parameter of ReferenceField
  * nested object is delegated to handle to `serializer.create` or `serializer.update`
