issue_title: create an outline / plan for a gcdt getting-started guide
issue_body:
**what would be the most useful gcdt getting-started guide**

write the outline from the perspective of a DevOps developer new to gcdt... what would be the most helpful.

probably a good idea to do a little research on google to see if some useful "getting started guides" come up on the topic of "AWS deployment" ... I do not think we have to reinvent the wheel if a guide with a perfect structure already exists - I believe the clever move would be to reuse a good and proven structure.

Please let us review the outline before you proceed to writing the actual getting-started guide. Please attach new revisions of the outline / plan to this issue if possible or submit as PR.

@jbrandstetter  on the topic of getting-started / supercars docu:

"the overall topic would be that after you understand all the different gcdt tools and their inner workings you should also be able to contribute code if the need comes up in your squad. also we’d like you to then start working on a detailed user’s guide from a beginner’s view. (quick start, tutorial, etc) because most of us already have some cognitive bias ;)"

**intended audience**

* developer
* knows AWS
* knows the service he / she is working on
* understands deployments in general

**requirements**

* use supercars as a sample (https://github.com/glomex/gcdt/issues/311)
* implement the `gcdt getting-started guide` as part of gcdt docs (http://gcdt.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)
* use the gcdt issues and Project kanban board to track progress (https://github.com/glomex/gcdt/projects/1)
* one of the most important things for the getting-started guide IMHO is the concept of infrastructure-as-code. Consequently it must put emphasis on this concept and revisit it frequently i.e. what it means in the context of kumo, tenkai, ramuda and yugen. I believe infrastructure-as-code is kind of a red-thread leading through the getting-started guide which shows how gcdt explicit infrastructure configuration makes it possible to apply the same tools and concepts we are  already familiar with and use to manage software and now apply them to working with infrastructure.
* please, in case you are missing some gcdt docu or the existing docu is incomplete or difficult to comprehend please drop a line with your suggestion into https://github.com/glomex/gcdt/issues/221
* on 30.06 we agreed to call this `Getting starated guide` and the file `03_getting_started.md`

issue_title: sio_magic native 3 conversion script
issue_body:
@Caoimhinmg, some odd things are happening in the sio_magic conversion.  I've been working with the data_files/Measurement_Import/sio_magic/sio_af_example.dat

Thing one:

It looks like specimen, sample, site, and location tables are all getting populated with a bunch of empty records.  The programs seems to be adding a record to SpecRecs for each MagRec, most of which are empty.  So, right before the specimen table is created, SpecRecs looks like this:

    SpecRecs [{'sample': 'sc12b1', 'specimen': 'sc12b1'}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}]

This then results in the MagIC tables being all wonky.  


Thing two:

Also, I made a few changes in how the contribution is created, so these lines aren't necessary:

    con.add_empty_magic_table('specimens')
    con.add_empty_magic_table('samples')
    con.add_empty_magic_table('sites')
    con.add_empty_magic_table('locations')
    con.add_empty_magic_table('measurements')

What that does is to add a table to the contribution of the proper type with default required headers and/or headers you specify (so that you can build up a table piece by piece with MagicDataFrame.add), but you are overwriting most of those defaults anyway by assigning df in these lines: 

    con.tables['specimens'].df = DataFrame(SpecRecs)
    # etc.

later.  

This ends up missing some stuff that should happen in the MagicDataFrame \_\_init\_\_, like making sure the index is set correctly.  To address this I added in a data parameter to the MagicDataFrame \_\_init\_\_.  So I changed sio_magic to add MagicDataFrames in a one-liner:

    con.tables['specimens'] = MagicDataFrame(dtype='specimens', data=SpecRecs)

This part two isn't an action item for you, I just wanted to explain what I've done.  I may do similar edits in some of the other conversion scripts as well.  Sorry that functionality was missing before, but I think that this should work better.  

I would appreciate your input on item one, though.

issue_title: agate-sql: csvsql: Specify precision and scale for DECIMAL
issue_body:
Given the following table:
```
ID | X | Ref_ID
1 | 42.4242424242 | 99900000002
```

Invoking `csvsql --dialect mysql --snifflimit 100000 datatwithheaders.csv > mytabledef.sql` on this table produces:
```
CREATE TABLE `Facility` (
	`ID` DECIMAL NOT NULL, 
	`X` DECIMAL, 
	`Ref_ID` DECIMAL NOT NULL, 
);
```

When importing the data into the Facility table, the values unexpectedly change to:
1 | 42 | 9999999999

This is because the create table function produced by csvsql neglected to specify the precision and scale of the decimals:
- 42.4242424242 changes to 42 because the type of this row is DECIMAL(10,0). Hence the decimal values are truncated. In this case, X is a geographic coordinate, so this column should have been created with DECIMAL(12,10)
- 99900000002 changes to 9999999999 because DECIMAL(10,0) is not large enough to hold this number.

issue_title: Starter does not terminate child processes properly
issue_body:
Currently, when `aetros run` is executed in non-docker mode, the following group (tree) of 3 processes are created :
- `aetros run`
  - `sh -c [command]`
    - `[command]` (for example `pip install -r requirements.txt && python script.py`)

The issue is that when the job is aborted, child processes (`sh` and `[command]`) are not terminated properly (with `SIGINT`).

To replicate it, consider the following script `terminate.py`:
```python
import signal
from time import sleep

import aetros.backend
from aetros.utils import prepend_signal_handler


def on_sigint(sig, frame):
    print("on_sigint called: %s" % sig)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    job = aetros.backend.context()
    prepend_signal_handler(signal.SIGINT, on_sigint)
    try:
        print("Starting..")
        for i in range(1000):
            sleep(2)
            print("i: %d" % i)
    except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit) as e:
        print("Process interrupted!", e)

```

If we run `python terminate.py`, then we will notice that when we terminate the job (i.e. press `Ctrl+C` or stop the job in the Trainer app, or via `kill -SIGINT`) we will notice that `on_sigint` was called and `SystemExit` exception was caught. (P.S. `SystemExit` exception is caught instead of `KeyboardInterrupt` because aetros backend terminates with sys.exit, see https://github.com/aetros/aetros-cli/blob/4281392260bf4a669475b384dd81e31ebc7b68c5/aetros/backend.py#L921)

However, when we run the same script with aetros starter (i.e. `aetros run --local`) and abort/interrupt the job by any of the above methods, you will notice that python process hasn't been terminated properly (particularly, you will see counting numbers after job status changed to aborted and stopped). Moreover, this behaviour might be more unpleasant if we have a child processes inside our python script. In such case, child process will not be terminated at all and keep running.

issue_title: RENN stopping criterion
issue_body:
There is an issue with RENN. The algorithm does not stop repeating when the imbalancing is already corrected leading to potential unique class data sampling.

For instance, taking:

```python
import numpy as np
from imblearn.under_sampling import  RepeatedEditedNearestNeighbours()

X = np.array([[ 2.59928271,  0.93323465],
              [ 0.25738379,  0.95564169],
              [ 1.42772181,  0.526027  ],
              [ 1.92365863,  0.82718767],
              [-0.10903849, -0.12085181],
              [-0.284881  , -0.62730973],
              [ 0.57062627,  1.19528323],
              [ 0.03394306,  0.03986753],
              [ 0.78318102,  2.59153329],
              [ 0.35831463,  1.33483198],
              [-0.14313184, -1.0412815 ],
              [ 0.01936241,  0.17799828],
              [-1.25020462, -0.40402054],
              [-0.09816301, -0.74662486],
              [-0.01252787,  0.34102657],
              [ 0.52726792, -0.38735648],
              [ 0.2821046 , -0.07862747],
              [ 0.05230552,  0.09043907],
              [ 0.15198585,  0.12512646],
              [ 0.70524765,  0.39816382]])
Y = np.array([1, 2, 1, 1, 0, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 0, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 1, 2, 1])

renn = RepeatedEditedNearestNeighbours()

x, y = renn.fit_sample(X, Y)
```

This will lead to: 

```
.../imblearn/base.py:93: RuntimeWarning: Only one class detected, something will get wrong
  RuntimeWarning)
```

Since that after some iteration there is only one class.
The stopping criterion need to be change such that we stop before that.

issue_title: Overriding function that return a reference lead to a compilation error
Meta: clarify the project scope and goals
issue_body:
With a growing number of users and contributors, we need to define clearly what the project goals are, and what we don't plan to handle.

### Current scope
As far as I'm concerned, the scope of the library is:
* Allow to use a LDAP service as a Django database backend, without Django noticing;
* Handle any form of LDAP layout that complies to the official RFCs;
* Don't assume anything on the LDAP layout: only use standard methods and queries;
* Support all supported Python versions, the latest stable Django release, and the latest Django LTS strictly before that release.

This means that we'll have to handle the following features:
* ``./manage.py inspectdb`` (based on the schema) => #155;
* Handle arbitrary tree structures => #3;
* Support or emulate transaction support => #83.

### Options to consider
I can think of a few extra options we might want to include in the future, or which could be handled in a separated library.

**Server-specific extensions**
We could add custom fields for specific directories, similar to ``django.contrib.postgres`` (for instance, a field to handle an ActiveDirectory ``SID`` or ``userAccountControl``).
If those fields require additional Python dependencies, we should likely move them to dedicated packages (still under the ``django-ldapdb`` organization).

**Account management helpers**
We could add a few helpers to be used when the Django account base is backed by the same LDAP we're talking to.
I'm not sure of the scope of that section yet; besides, this looks like the goal of the [django-auth-ldap](https://django-auth-ldap.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) project.

**Specific form fields**
A LDAP supports multi-valued fields. We might want to provide a clean type of widget for such fields in the admin and other ``ModelForm``s.
However, getting a clean user experience for a multi-valued form field is complex; we might want to look at existing Django field libraries to reuse some.
Or maybe push those changes to a side library, since it should be compatible with any database where a field supports a collection of values.

**More?**
*Do you see other features we could add?*

issue_title: Make auth = basic the default again
issue_body:
`auth = guess`, as implemented in #57 (#33), was a bad idea to have as default:

* It causes performance issues and confusing server logs, as in #293, #446 
* Apparently Darwin Calendar Server breaks with anything but preemptive auth, see #457 

@geier I'm not sure if I ever asked you this, but is there a particular server you know of that doesn't support preemptive basic auth?

I plan to ask on the CalConnect mailing list on what the best client behavior is.

issue_title: Collection names have to be guessed by the user
issue_body:
The concept of a "collection name" is not exposed through UIs outside of vdirsyncer. Consequently the user doesn't know a thing about them, and when wanting to explicitly define them (i.e. not just use `["from a", "from b"]`), they are left guessing.

#421 was an attempt at fixing this, but I think we could do better:

## Select by displayname

    [pair foo]
    # both mean the same thing
    collections = ["displayname/Marco's work calendar"]
    collections = ["work"]
   
## Have a command for listing collections

`vdirsyncer discover --list foo` would list all collection names including displayname. Then you can just copypaste them into the config

issue_title: save_successor fixes now lose renewer options
issue_body:
After the recent fixes to `save_successor` to make it update renewal config files, we now lose renewer options (not renewalparams) such as `renew_before_expiry`.

We weren't very worried about this because the use of these options isn't documented or encouraged anywhere; probably everyone using them is directly involved with the development of the project, or so we hope. It would be good for the code that updates the renewal config files to not lose these parameters, though!

issue_title: Ensure we have an assertion about all types parsed from the protocol
issue_labels: [{'id': 277074172, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20acme', 'name': 'area: acme', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 214927649, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20security', 'name': 'area: security', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 175439992, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/low%20priority', 'name': 'low priority', 'color': 'bfe5bf', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Once upon a time, we used JSON schemata to constrain incoming protocol messages, but RESTification and other protocol changes have made that unsustainable for the time being.

Instead, we should ensure that we have minimal type assertions around all values extracted from protocol JSON blobs.

## Dockerfile for building the image:
```
# sudo docker run -p 80:80 -p 443:443 -d -t --name PrivateBin -h samot.ninja -i privatebin
# Build with:
# docker build PrivateBin -t privatebin
FROM php:7.0-apache
EXPOSE 80
EXPOSE 443

ENV PRIVATEBIN_URL https://github.com/PrivateBin/PrivateBin/archive/master.zip

ADD cert.sh /cert.sh
RUN chmod a+x /cert.sh

RUN apt-get update
RUN apt-get install -y wget
RUN apt-get install -y unzip
RUN apt-get install -y cron

RUN set -x \
  && cd /var/www/html \
  && rm -rf *

RUN cd /tmp \
    && wget $PRIVATEBIN_URL \
    && unzip master.zip \
    && cd PrivateBin-master \
    && mv * /var/www/html \
    && cd /var/www/html

RUN echo 'deb http://ftp.debian.org/debian jessie-backports main' >> /etc/apt/sources.list
RUN apt-get update
RUN apt-get install -y python-certbot-apache -t jessie-backports

RUN rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

RUN echo "************************************************************************************************"
RUN echo "Don't forget to edit & run /cert.sh"
```

issue_title: [nginx] Failed to obtain certificate: "tried to insert directive"
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
```
$ uname -a
Linux dev 4.10.13-1-ARCH #1 SMP PREEMPT Thu Apr 27 12:15:09 CEST 2017 x86_64 GNU/Linux
$ pacman -Q | grep certbot
certbot 0.14.0-1
certbot-nginx 0.14.0-1
```

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
Arch Linux's `pacman`


## I ran this command and it produced this output:
```
$ sudo certbot --nginx -d www.getfambit.com,getfambit.com

issue_title: Setting up issue
issue_body: Hi, I'm relatively new to proxy servers so I apologize for my ignorance on the subject and ignorance on using this package. I came to `nbserverproxy` via the [`dask-jobqueue`](https://github.com/dask/dask-jobqueue) project. I am hoping to tunnel my notebook from the HPC to my local machine (laptop) and also tunnel the [`dask dashboard` ](http://dask.pydata.org/en/latest/diagnostics-distributed.html#dashboard) which is typically served at http://localhost:8787/status. I'm having trouble accessing the dashboard and `nbserverproxy` was proposed as a solution. Unfortunately, I couldn't get `nbserverproxy` to work and I was wondering if I was doing something wrong in the setup? The original issue is posted at https://github.com/dask/dask-jobqueue/issues/116 but I realized I should probably ask here for help with `nbserverproxy` rather than the guys in `dask-jobqueue`. I have an example below where I am trying to get the [README example](https://github.com/jupyterhub/nbserverproxy#example) to work ``` (djq_lsf) [rxb826@pegasus ~]$ conda install -c conda-forge nbserverproxy (djq_lsf) [rxb826@pegasus ~]$ jupyter serverextension enable --py nbserverproxy Enabling: nbserverproxy - Writing config: /nethome/rxb826/.jupyter - Validating... nbserverproxy OK (djq_lsf) [rxb826@pegasus ~]$ jupyter lab --no-browser [I 00:13:06.932 LabApp] The Jupyter Notebook is running at: [I 00:13:06.932 LabApp] http://localhost:8888/?token=... ``` Open http://localhost:8888 on my laptop and try the [README example](https://github.com/jupyterhub/nbserverproxy#example)

issue_title: Check for global variables
issue_body: Global variables shouldn't be used in CSC108/148. We should check for both top-level variables which are not constants, and forbid uses of the `global` and `nonlocal` keywords.

issue_title: Does nfcpy always polls when waiting for a NFC tag?
issue_body:
We use the following code to wait for a NFC tag and we are unsure how nfcpy handles this underneath...
```
import nfc

def connected(tag):
    """ Print the NFC Tag information"""

clf = nfc.ContactlessFrontend('tty:AMA0:pn532')

timeOut = lambda: time.time() - started > (5 * 60) 
started = time.time()
nfcTag = clf.connect(rdwr={'on-connect': connected}, terminate=timeOut)

""" Do something with the tag here"""
```
We are concern that nfcpy always poll waiting for a tag, thus the power consumption on our device is not optional.

Any feedback will be really appreciated...

issue_title: NettingChannelContract deposit critical issue. Likely to result in loss of funds for Raiden users.
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Raiden netting channel contract is a special contract that is designed to work with token deposits.

It requires users to send tokens to this contract using [deposit](https://github.com/EthereumCommonwealth/raiden/blob/master/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol#L67) function which will call the `transferFrom` function of the desired ERC-20 token.

ERC-20 token standard suffers critical security problem: it has two ways to transfer funds (1) [transfer](https://github.com/EthereumCommonwealth/raiden/blob/SafeRaiden/raiden/smart_contracts/Token.sol#L16) function and (2) [approve + transferFrom](https://github.com/EthereumCommonwealth/raiden/blob/SafeRaiden/raiden/smart_contracts/Token.sol#L23,L29) mechanism. The first method is usual method to transfer/deposit tokens for most users and third party services.

If the user deposits the ERC-20 tokens into the netting channel contract using the `transfer` function, the tokens will get stuck inside your contract without any possibility to recover them. **Which means that it will result in loss of money for Raiden users.**

You can read more about ERC20 critical problems [here](https://medium.com/@dexaran820/erc20-token-standard-critical-problems-3c10fd48657b). [Here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Feh5sP6oQL1-1NHi-X1dbgT3ch2WdhbXRevDN681Jv4/edit) you can find a small presentation as well.

Each contract that does not allow to extract ERC20 tokens will be a potential token trap. This problem already results in loss of more than $400,000. ([Read this](https://www.reddit.com/r/ethereum/comments/6e8y9o/the_ens_contract_becomes_token_holder_erc20/) or [this](https://www.reddit.com/r/ethereum/comments/6uckdx/reminder_about_problems_of_most_erc20_tokens/) for example).

Again, relying on ERC20 token `transferFrom` deposit mechanism is guaranteed to lead to a loss of money for your users.

## Solution

You **must** reject accidental token `transfer`s by analogy with how the contract rejects accidental Ether transfers. Unfortunately, you can't because of ERC20 token suffers one more critical problem: lack of [event handling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Event_(computing)) mechanism (transaction is event in this case).

There are two ways to avoid the described problem:

1. Implement a special function that will allow a centralized trusted party to extract any stuck ERC20 token from netting channel by default. In this case, you will likely act as trusted party and you should refund all the users manually. 

https://github.com/EthereumCommonwealth/raiden/blob/SafeRaiden/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol#L434-L438

2. Do not work with ERC20 tokens at all. Personally I think that relying on mechanism that is guaranteed to result in loss of funds for your users is not a good idea.

issue_title: Get rid of local signing
issue_body:
Related to https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/1400#issuecomment-387235851

At the moment we always sign transactions locally on Raiden and send it to the ethereum node for transmission. That is introducing some friction on determining the order of transactions and on handling the possibility of other applications using the same private key that Raiden uses.

The alternative would be to simply delegate the transaction signing to the ethereum node and use something like `eth_sendTransaction` in order to send Transactions to the node and let it do the nonce management.


This issue needs discussion.

We need to evaluate the reason we use local signing, compare with letting the ethereum node do the signing and nonce management and then if it makes sense to change the current behaviour to do so.

issue_title: Prevent BalanceProof replay attacks for different Registries and Ethereum networks
issue_body:
With #287 we implement a [replay attack](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/39) mitigation scheme, that relies on the state of one (and only one) raiden network. We assume, that a channel will have a limited number of messages per lifetime and apply a starting nonce scheme, based on the block number.

Since anyone can start a new raiden network by just deploying another instance of the `Registry` contract, there is no guarantee that Alice and Bob will never have a channel for the same asset opened at the same block number in two raiden networks `R` and `R'` (which would lead to the same nonce ranges being valid in channel `AB(R)` and `AB(R')`).

### Level One State Specific Replay Attack Mitigation

A state specific replay attack mitigation scheme would (instead of relying on the opening block number) use something specific to the channel contract instance, e.g. hashing the channel contract address into each signature.

Note, that this potentially still leaves replay attack vectors open for raiden instances on different blockchains (i.e. testnet vs mainnet), across forks, etc...

### Level Two State Specific Replay Attack Mitigation

For further hardening, the opening party could provide a blockchain specific piece of information (e.g. genesis block hash/fork block hash) that would become part of the channel data and should be concatenated with the channel contract address for signatures.

Whether we need this second level of replay protection should be discussed.

See also #93

issue_title: New "Configuration Parameter"
issue_body:
Based on a discussion with @damazter I would like to put forward the concept of a configuration parameter. 

What is a configuration parameter? 
* Behaves like a manual parameter but if it gets set changes flag `_configure_required` to `True`. 
* Some other parameters call a `configure()` method whenever `_configure_required==True`. 

What problem does this solve? 
* Some instruments require configuration before a measurement can be started. Examples include the ATS driver and the Signal Hound.  The configure command usually uses a set of parameters to at once upload/set some configuration. 
* The configuration parameter allows interacting with all parameters of the instrument as if they are normal parameters while simultaneously ensuring that the instrument gets reconfigured whenever required. 

I think this is a sufficiently common problem that it warrants a new parameter class. The current instruments that need it both have a different solution. 

The [signal hound driver](https://github.com/QCoDeS/Qcodes/blob/master/qcodes/instrument_drivers/signal_hound/USB_SA124B.py) solves this problem by using manual parameters and the requiring the user to call the configure command by hand. The ill documentation of this functionality, combined with the missing of a simple getable parameter that represents a measurement (based on my shoddy port of an existing qtlab driver) is what initiated this discussion. 

The [ATS driver](https://github.com/QCoDeS/Qcodes/blob/master/qcodes/instrument_drivers/AlazarTech/ATS.py) solves this problem by introducing an unsetable `AlazarParameter` that can only be changed using either the private method or the configure command. 

I would argue that the concept of a configuration parameter is the prefered solution to this problem. However, I explicitly put this forward as a point of discussion as introducing a new concept must not be done lightly. 

( @giulioungaretti, Probably intentional but I am no longer able to apply labels to issues, would make it easier to structure things a bit.)

P.S. as this issue was motivated by the low quality of a driver that I ported this raises a separate issue as to who will work on improving existing drivers, maybe an interesting subject for a discussion on Slack.

issue_title: validate_next issue with pytorch 0.4
issue_body:
I discovered an issue while trying to use `lr_find2` with pytorch 0.4.
`lr_find2` calls `validate_next`, in which we have the following:
```
1. def validate_next(stepper, metrics, val_iter):
2.     """Computes the loss on the next minibatch of the validation set."""
3.     stepper.reset(False)
4.     import pdb; pdb.set_trace()
5.     with no_grad_context():
6.         (*x,y) = val_iter.next()
7.         preds,l = stepper.evaluate(VV(x), VV(y))
8.         res = [to_np(l)[0]]
9.         res += [f(preds.data,y) for f in metrics]
10.     stepper.reset(True)
11.     return res
```

I get an IndexError at line 8. The issue is that in pytorch 0.4 `l` is a tensor:
```
(Pdb) !l
tensor(8.2462, device='cuda:0')
```
and when `to_np(l)` is called the following is returned:
`(Pdb) to_np(l)
array(8.24617, dtype=float32)`

This array is scalar and cannot be indexed. In older versions of pytorch I saw the following behavior.

```
(Pdb) !l
Variable containing:
 8.2459
[torch.cuda.FloatTensor of size 1 (GPU 0)]

(Pdb) [to_np(l)[0]]
[8.245869]
```

I am not yet familiar enough with the library and recent changes to pytorch to recommend a solution, but I would appreciate any input into this problem.

issue_title: Fine-tuning inception v3 - low learning rate
[feature proposal] Composing transformations with __add__ magic method
issue_body:
TLDR: `transform = Resize((299, 299)) + ToTensor()`

## Motivation

Consider the common use case of building an overall-common-but-differing-at-one-point transformation pipeline for train and test sets, for instance applying augmentation transforms only to train set:
```python
train_transform = transforms.Compose([
    Resize((299, 299), interpolation=1),
    RandomCrop(299, padding=4),
    RandomHorizontalFlip(),
    RandomGrayscale(),
    ToTensor(), 
    Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225])
])

test_transform = transforms.Compose([
    Resize((299, 299), interpolation=1), 
    ToTensor(), 
    Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225])
])
```

A way of expressing of `train_transform` builds upon `test_transform` would be handy. What you could do now is to recursively use `transforms.Compose` (i.e. `Compose([Compose(...resizing...), Compose(...augmentation...], Normalize(...)])`), but I don't find this particularly clean (and intuitively, final transforms objects should be sequential objects with only one level of depth).

## Proposed behavior
```python
common_transforms = ToTensor() + Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225])
augmentation = RandomCrop(299, padding=4) + RandomHorizontalFlip() + RandomGrayscale()
train_transforms =  Resize((299, 299)) + augmentation + common_transforms
test_transforms = Resize((299, 299)) + common_transforms
```

Note how this mimics the behavior of Python lists (and other iterables):
```python
>>> [1, 2] + [3, 4]
[1, 2, 3, 4]
```

## Implementation sketch

```python
class Transform(object):
    # Right now all transforms directly inherit from object. 
    # Let Transform be a superclass for all transformations

    def __call__(self, pic):
        raise NotImplementedError('Each subclass should implement this method')

    def __repr__(self):
        raise NotImplementedError('Each subclass should implement this method')

    def __add__(self, other):
        if not isinstance(other, Transform):
            raise TypeError('Only transformations can be added')
        if isinstance(self, Compose) and isinstance(other, Compose):
            return Compose(self.transforms + other.transforms)
        if not isinstance(self, Compose) and isinstance(other, Compose):
            other.transforms = self + other.transforms
            return other
        if isinstance(self, Compose) and not isinstance(other, Compose):
            self.transforms = self.transforms + other
            return self
        if not isinstance(self, Compose) and not isinstance(other, Compose):
            return Compose([self, other])
```

Comments are most welcome!

issue_title: In storage.py  get_users_by_email()  not working correctly if there is inactive accounts
Improve performance of pulumi preview
issue_body:
Running `pulumi { preview, update, destroy }` makes a lot of API calls to the Pulumi Service, such as every time a log line is rendered, and for every change that is made to the checkpoint file.

The latency added by each API call update process can add many seconds to the overall update. Most of the time this is masked by the much longer delay in actually modifying cloud resources. But for previews which are persisted in the service, it can increase the duration by a large factor.

This issue tracks improving the performance of `pulumi preview` with regard to the overhead of communicating with the service.

issue_title: Remove hardcoded knowledge of serialized-functions from plan.go diffing code.
issue_body:
As part of https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/pull/591 we hardcode in some knowledge of what serialized functions look like, so we can adjust the text for a better diffing experience.

It would be better if this was not in this part of the codebase.  instead, ideally, the resource could specify what the 'massaged text' was that it wanted the plan code to work with.

issue_title: Order of pulumi new args could be improved
issue_body:
We got feedback about the `pulumi new` command and, after looking at the sequence of steps that led to this feedback, it occurs to me that the ordering of arguments, etc. is a bit off from what one might expect. The user who reported this tried `pulumi new <project-name>` which, to be honest, is a very reasonable assumption (probably more so than assuming `pulumi new <template>`, which is what we do today). In fact, we later added the `--dir` flag, but it's not at all evident, and is probably what most users will want to do anyway (all of our examples are slowly switching to using it).

A better flow would probably be to

* Use `pulumi new <project-name>`, instead of `pulumi new <template>`
* By default, prompt for the template, as we do today
* Support a `--template` flag for selecting the template if you know it in advance
* Behave as though `--dir <project-name>` were passed by default; if you want the current behavior of running in the current working directory -- probably rare -- you can just do `--dir .`

issue_title: Does numbuf make sense as part of pyarrow?
issue_body:
Numbuf (under `src/numbuf`) is a library for serializing more general Python objects using Arrow.

Questions:

1. Would this make sense as part of pyarrow?
2. Would it be realistic to build a Java equivalent of numbuf?

issue_title: [tune] Add option to checkpoint model at end of training
issue_body:
### Describe the problem
Currently we checkpoint models only periodically. However this might not coincide with the exact end of training. It would also be useful to add an option to checkpoint them while terminating a trial, so that the user can retrieve the final parameters as well as the final score.

issue_title: process_challenge() raises NotAuthorizedException when signing in with alias from clients with secret
issue_body:
https://github.com/capless/warrant/blob/60b9cc0f7ccd4a9a37dc93b1bf6bdc3705981149/warrant/aws_srp.py#L199
This results in an error `botocore.errorfactory.NotAuthorizedException: ... Unable to verify secret hash for client <client_id>` if a user is trying to authenticate using not the ultimate username, but an alias (such as `email`, `phone_number` or `preferred_username`). Is seems that AWS Cognito always compares SECRET_HASH against the one calculated based on ultimate username, regardless of whether an alias was used to initiate the login or not. Modifying it to `self.get_secret_hash(user_id_for_srp, self.client_id, self.client_secret)` seems to resolve the issue.

issue_title: get_default_state outputs quantities with wrong dimensions
issue_body:
The output of get_default_state is zero dimensional (only a scalar) if the field is uniform in all directions.
While this is ok for components since the extracted arrays are in the correct shape, the Monitors don't
seem to get the state in the correct shape, and therefore if you do a `store` at the first step, the dimensions
are not correct.

I think the correct approach would be to ensure that the initial state itself has the correct dimensions. This is also important to not throw off anyone inspecting the initial state.

issue_title: side effects of text writing outside text widget input space
issue_body:
I am coding an ipywidget GUI with Text widgets in a jupyter cell. Pretty standard, I suppose.
 
Whenever, as a user of the GUI,, I position the mouse slightly outside the text input, I end up overwriting the cell. Then typically jupyter automatically switches the cell from code to Markdown, and the GUI disappears.

This would be nasty for a distributed software. I explored the Readonly nbextension to overcome the problem, but I do not see how  to package it for an easy software installation. 

I wonder if others find the same problem and if there can be an internal ipywidget solution, such as disabling the keyboard input from outside the appropriate widgets.

Thanks a lot

Roberto

issue_title: repo doesn't have URL (linking to docs, ideally)
What should happen if a task is cancelled before it begins executing?
issue_body:
Currently, if a task is spawned and then immediately cancelled, we begin executing the task, with a note that `Cancelled` should be raised the first time it blocks. C# does it differently: if a task is cancelled before it begins executing, then it just never begins executing. Effectively there is an implicit cancellation point just before the task begins executing.

I feel like there is some reason why I did it this way, but I don't remember what that is :-). We should at least write down the rationale somewhere for the historical record...

One place where this makes a difference: Currently, `spawn(fn, *args)` calls `fn(*args)` immediately, within the caller's scope. Because `fn` is async, this doesn't actually start executing the code, but it does mean that errors like "fn is not callable" or "fn takes 3 arguments (2 given)" get raised immediately within the caller. It also means that we create the coroutine object immediately when `spawn` is called, and once we create the coroutine object we are committed to executing it, because otherwise Python will whine about "coroutine was never awaited". So if we switched to allowing pre-emptive cancellation to prevent task execution entirely, then we would also have to delay calling `fn(*args)` until just before we pumped the task for the first time. Doable, but definitely some extra complexity, and a change in user-visible semantics.

issue_title: trio_asyncio: needs a hook for "await future" et al.
issue_body:
Over in https://github.com/vxgmichel/aiostream/issues/17#issuecomment-408337365 …:

> @smurfix the yield here is really an await, and trio-asyncio certainly has to intercept those or else await future wouldn't work :-)

Funny you should say that because, well, it doesn't. Not in native mode, anyway. (Compatibility mode nicely papered over this large crack.) The fun part is that outside the asyncio testsuite "await future" appears to be a really uncommon thing to do – otherwise we'd have run into that problem already.

Adding an intrusive interceptor for awaitables that trio doesn't understand would slow down the whole thing like crazy. So, could we instead think about adding an appropriate hook to Trio? Stored in the task's contextvar?

A sync procedure that gets the returned object and the Trio task, and returns a proper Trio `WaitTaskRescheduled` object, would probably work. I'll test that.

issue_title: Implement local file staging
issue_body:
Currently, in order to have parsl transfer a local file for you, you must either:

1) set up an FTP server locally and use File(’ftp:...),
2) set up an HTTPS server locally and use File(’https:...),
3) set up a GridFTP server locally (e.g. Globus Connect Server or Globus Connect Personal) and use File(’globus:...).

We need a simple way of transferring a file that exists locally which does not require setting up a server. Probably using `channel.push_file` and `channel.pull_file` would cover 90% of our use cases (the caveat would be it wouldn't work if workers did not have shared filesystem access). It also has the advantage of already being authenticated.

issue_title: Dynamically load of python scripts in Java from String
issue_body:
Looking at the API from Jep in Java, I've realized that it's not possible to load dynamically a script from a string. Of course, there are multiple alternatives to accomplished this:
1. As #55 suggested, use `eval('exec _pythonScript')`
2. Write temporal files to disk
3. Wrap the script as a function, and use `invoke()` if function was previously loaded. How?
4. Provide a `runScript(InputStream)` or `runScript(String code)` with the code.

I believe that the last options could improve the performance since to interactive mode is required. What is the best way to accomplish this?

issue_title: Firehose to Elastisearch stream can't be created
issue_body:
<!-- Love localstack? Please consider supporting our collective:
👉  https://opencollective.com/localstack/donate -->

I am running localstack on Docker and using CLI scripts to start up a firehose to s3 and elasticsearch stream. The S3 destination gets created and works like a charm. 

However, the elasticsearch destination is never created and ignored completely. There were no errors coming up either.

The elasticsearch configuration:
 ``` 
 {
  "RoleARN": "arn:aws:iam::000000000000:role/firehose",
  "DomainARN": "arn:aws:es:us-east-1:000000000000:domain/audit-logs-bkbn-default",
  "IndexName": "audit-log-bkbn",
  "TypeName": "bkbn",
  "IndexRotationPeriod": "OneMonth",
  "S3BackupMode": "AllDocuments",
  "S3Configuration": {
    "RoleARN": "arn:aws:iam::000000000000:role/firehose",
    "BucketARN": "arn:aws:s3:::efk-bucket-backbone-default",
    "BufferingHints": {
      "SizeInMBs": 10,
      "IntervalInSeconds": 400
    },
    "CompressionFormat": "UNCOMPRESSED"
  }
}
```

The command run for creating firehose stream:

```
aws --endpoint-url=${URL}:${FIREHOSE_PORT} firehose create-delivery-stream \
      --delivery-stream-name audit-stream-backbone-default \
      --elasticsearch-destination-configuration file://./es.json
```


The end result:

```
$ aws --endpoint-url=http://192.168.99.100:4573 firehose describe-delivery-stream --delivery-stream-name audit-stream-backbone-default
{
    "DeliveryStreamDescription": {
        "DeliveryStreamName": "audit-stream-backbone-default",
        "DeliveryStreamARN": "arn:aws:firehose:us-east-1:000000000000:deliverystream/audit-stream-backbone-default",
        "DeliveryStreamStatus": "ACTIVE",
        "VersionId": "1",
        "CreateTimestamp": 1515757815.738184,
        "Destinations": [],
        "HasMoreDestinations": false
    }
}
```

issue_title: S3 DELETE incorrectly returns a nonzero Content-Length in 0.8.4
issue_body:
In localstack 0.8.3, this call:

`curl -i -X DELETE http://localstack:4572/bucketname/objectname`

Would correctly return:

```
HTTP/1.1 204 No Content
Server: BaseHTTP/0.3 Python/2.7.13
Date: Sat, 06 Jan 2018 19:25:50 GMT
Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
Content-Length: 0
Server: Werkzeug/0.14.1 Python/2.7.13
Date: Sat, 06 Jan 2018 19:25:50 GMT
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *
Access-Control-Allow-Methods: HEAD,GET,PUT,POST,DELETE,OPTIONS,PATCH
Access-Control-Allow-Headers: authorization,content-type,content-md5,x-amz-content-sha256,x-amz-date,x-amz-security-token,x-amz-user-agent
```

Starting with 0.8.4, the same call returns:

```
HTTP/1.1 204 No Content
Server: BaseHTTP/0.3 Python/2.7.13
Date: Sat, 06 Jan 2018 19:25:50 GMT
Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
Content-Length: 199
Server: Werkzeug/0.14.1 Python/2.7.13
Date: Sat, 06 Jan 2018 19:25:50 GMT
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *
Access-Control-Allow-Methods: HEAD,GET,PUT,POST,DELETE,OPTIONS,PATCH
Access-Control-Allow-Headers: authorization,content-type,content-md5,x-amz-content-sha256,x-amz-date,x-amz-security-token,x-amz-user-agent
```

Note the spurious **Content-Length: 199** causes an exception in the Seahorse library used by the ruby aws-sdk:

`Seahorse::Client::NetworkingError: http response body truncated, expected 199 bytes, received 0 bytes`
  
This is blocking our tests and so we have had to revert back to 0.8.3 for now.

issue_title: Results not on a regular time grid

from fmpy import simulate_fmu

filename = 'fmus/Dymola2017/CoupledClutches.fmu'

issue_title: throw out full overlaps
Not escaping strings?
issue_body:
So I found out, that on some places, strings are not escaped, before they are sent to server.
For example: In my company, we have a policy, which states, that new feature branches, must follow naming scheme `feature/{name of the feature}`. 

Creating branches using this library works as expected, but deletion is a different matter.

So in my script, I call something like this:

    gitlab.v4.objects.Project.branches.get('feature/branch_name').delete()

And I got 404:

    File "/home/tomas_capek/.virtualenvs/mrobot-latest/lib/python3.6/site-packages/gitlab/exceptions.py", line 239, in wrapped_f
     return f(*args, **kwargs)
    File "/home/tomas_capek/.virtualenvs/mrobot-latest/lib/python3.6/site-packages/gitlab/mixins.py", line 239, in delete
      self.gitlab.http_delete(path, **kwargs)
    File "/home/tomas_capek/.virtualenvs/mrobot-latest/lib/python3.6/site-packages/gitlab/__init__.py", line 842, in http_delete
      return self.http_request('delete', path, **kwargs)
    File "/home/tomas_capek/.virtualenvs/mrobot-latest/lib/python3.6/site-packages/gitlab/__init__.py", line 700, in http_request
      response_body=result.content)
    gitlab.exceptions.GitlabHttpError: 404: b'{"error":"404 Not Found"}'

So upon a close inspection, it is not surprising. API request was not escaped:

    DELETE /api/v4/projects/42/repository/branches/feature/branches HTTP/1.1" 404 None

Can somebody fix this please?

issue_title: NNVM Compiler and Interpolation
issue_body:
Last month we saw the splashing release of onnx, which I think personally think is a great step toward common model exchange format of deep learning frameworks. I am glad that the community has been moving toward more interpolation and collaboration. First in case of [dlpack](https://github.com/dmlc/dlpack) for tensor structure and now in terms of model exchange. I also see a lot of common spirits when we develop NNVM. 

We did not immediately jump into discussion issues because we think we should bring something to the community that can work with common exchange formats and focus on graph and tensor level optimization, thus the  [NNVM Compiler](http://www.tvmlang.org/2017/10/06/nnvm-compiler-announcement.html).

We would like now to jump in and discuss potential ways to work together. In the very least, now nnvm compiler provides an end to end solution to bring onnx to bare metal. As I mentioned to  @soumith and @Yangqing personally before, I would like to see more interpolations happen and make nnvm compiler and onnx to work together to benefit the users.

Technically there is a barrier to code migration, barrier of the institutions or barrier of pride. But I think at least we can have an openminded discussion on what each member of the community think the exchange layer and compilation layer should look like, for example

- Serialized IR(onnx) for exchange v.s. in memory IR(nnvm or pytorch's jit ir) for graph optimization
- What can nnvm learn from onnx, vice versa
- What should be good in-memory structure for graph IR
- What is a good strategy for operator extension
- Should numpy be supported as a basic primitive or lower level op for ease of vendor implementation

Through the discussion maybe there would be more interesting ways of interpolation and lessons learned.

issue_title: IOC fanger should support completely offline operation
issue_body:
* IOC Fanger version: 1.0.0

### Description

As it is `import ioc_fanger` results in a 2 request to be done to an API, which serves a number of find/replace values, which are later used in all subquentent calls to `ioc_fanger.fang` and `ioc_fanger.defang`.

Due to this - and inability to supply offline copy of this very small data sets (both have only 2 elements at the moment) we had to throw the ioc_fanger out of our dependencies.

Problems we had with the current solution:
- in case of https://ioc-fang.github.io/defanging-dataset/ downtime our project would crash and burn on launch when `import ioc_fanger` is called
- rules could be changed at any time without as knowing
 - inconsistent output of the defang/fang with the one from the past
 - malicious rules could be entered (either DoS our application, or replacing every link with fanged link to something malicous during defang - there is number of possibilities here)

Suggested solution of this issue:
- replace load of dataset on import with lazy load on first use
- allow preloading rule dataset, which should prevent outgoing request altogether
